stem: A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 6 weeks of amenorrhoea, mild left iliac fossa pain, and light vaginal spotting. She is haemodynamically stable. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a 22 mm left adnexal mass with no cardiac activity and an empty uterus. There is no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16, Temp 36.8 degrees C
data_table.Investigations: Serum beta-hCG 2800 IU/L, Hb 128 g/L, platelets 245 x10^9/L, creatinine 62 micromol/L, ALT 22 U/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 with serial beta-hCG monitoring on days 4 and 7
options.rationale: This patient meets all criteria for medical management of ectopic pregnancy: haemodynamically stable, unruptured ectopic less than 35 mm without cardiac activity, beta-hCG less than 5000 IU/L, normal renal and hepatic function, and adequate platelet count. Single-dose methotrexate is first-line in this setting per RANZCOG guidelines. (Source: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

options.text: Multi-dose intramuscular methotrexate protocol with alternating leucovorin rescue over 8 days
options.rationale: Multi-dose methotrexate is reserved for cases with higher beta-hCG levels (typically above 5000 IU/L) or where single-dose treatment has failed. This patient's beta-hCG of 2800 IU/L and small ectopic make single-dose the appropriate first choice. (Source: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingectomy with histological confirmation of products of conception
options.rationale: Surgical management is appropriate for ruptured ectopic, haemodynamic instability, contraindications to methotrexate, or patient preference. This stable patient with a small ectopic and low beta-hCG is an ideal candidate for medical management, which avoids surgical morbidity. (Source: KEMH Clinical Guidelines - Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Expectant management with serial beta-hCG levels every 48 hours until resolution confirmed
options.rationale: Expectant management may be considered when beta-hCG is very low (typically below 1000-1500 IU/L) and declining, with minimal symptoms and a small or non-visualised ectopic. With a beta-hCG of 2800 IU/L and a visible adnexal mass, active treatment with methotrexate is preferred. (Source: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingostomy with postoperative serial beta-hCG monitoring to exclusion
options.rationale: Salpingostomy (tube-conserving surgery) is considered when the contralateral tube is absent or damaged and the patient desires future fertility. It requires postoperative beta-hCG surveillance for persistent trophoblast. With both tubes intact and medical eligibility, methotrexate is preferred over any surgical approach. (Source: KEMH Clinical Guidelines - Ectopic Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Medical management with single-dose methotrexate is first-line for unruptured ectopic pregnancy when eligibility criteria are met: haemodynamic stability, no cardiac activity, ectopic less than 35 mm, beta-hCG below 5000 IU/L, and normal haematological, renal, and hepatic parameters. Success rates exceed 85% in appropriately selected patients. Follow-up involves beta-hCG measurement on days 4 and 7, with expectation of at least a 15% decline between those values. Failure of adequate decline mandates repeat dosing or surgical intervention.
key_points: Single-dose methotrexate success rate exceeds 85% when beta-hCG is below 5000 IU/L
A minimum 15% decline in beta-hCG between day 4 and day 7 indicates treatment success
Contraindications to methotrexate include hepatic or renal impairment, thrombocytopaenia, breastfeeding, and immunodeficiency
Patients must avoid folate supplements, NSAIDs, and conception for at least 3 months post-treatment
explanation.pearls: After single-dose methotrexate for ectopic pregnancy, a transient rise in beta-hCG between days 1 and 4 is expected and does not indicate treatment failure - only the day 4 to day 7 decline of at least 15% determines success.

sources.label: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

stem: A 32-year-old woman at 9 weeks gestation presents with heavy vaginal bleeding and cramping lower abdominal pain for 4 hours. On speculum examination, the cervical os is open with tissue visible at the os. She has soaked through four pads in 2 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102 bpm, BP 105/68 mmHg, RR 18, Temp 36.9 degrees C
data_table.Investigations: Hb 108 g/L (booking Hb 132 g/L), blood group A Rh-negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral mifepristone 200 mg now followed by vaginal misoprostol 800 mcg in 48 hours
options.rationale: Medical management with mifepristone-misoprostol is appropriate for missed or incomplete miscarriage when the patient is haemodynamically stable without tissue at the os. With active heavy bleeding and visible tissue at the cervical os, immediate removal of the tissue is required to allow the cervix to close and stop bleeding. (Source: Queensland Health - Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Remove visible tissue from the cervical os with sponge forceps and arrange surgical evacuation
options.rationale: Tissue lodged at the cervical os causes cervical shock (vasovagal from cervical distension) and prevents uterine contraction, perpetuating bleeding. Immediate removal of cervical os tissue with sponge forceps is the priority, followed by definitive surgical evacuation (suction curettage) given the heavy ongoing bleeding. Anti-D immunoglobulin is also required given her Rh-negative status. (Source: RANZCOG - Management of Early Pregnancy Loss)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Management of Early Pregnancy Loss

options.text: Establish IV access with fluid resuscitation and arrange urgent transvaginal ultrasound first
options.rationale: While IV access and fluid resuscitation are appropriate concurrent steps, delaying removal of tissue at the os to obtain an ultrasound is not indicated. The clinical picture is clear: open os with visible tissue and heavy bleeding. The immediate priority is removing the obstructing tissue. (Source: Queensland Health - Early Pregnancy Loss)

options.text: Administer IV oxytocin 10 units and observe for spontaneous expulsion over the next 4 hours
options.rationale: Oxytocin may assist uterine contraction but does not address the immediate problem of tissue at the cervical os preventing cervical closure. Waiting for spontaneous expulsion risks worsening haemorrhage and haemodynamic compromise. Active removal is required. (Source: RANZCOG - Management of Early Pregnancy Loss)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Management of Early Pregnancy Loss

options.text: Administer intramuscular tranexamic acid 1 g and vaginal misoprostol 400 mcg sublingually
options.rationale: While tranexamic acid reduces bleeding in various settings, it does not address the mechanical cause of ongoing haemorrhage - tissue at the cervical os preventing uterine contraction. Sublingual misoprostol alone is insufficient when tissue is obstructing the os. (Source: Queensland Health - Early Pregnancy Loss)

explanation.summary: Tissue lodged at the cervical os is a gynaecological emergency that requires immediate manual removal. The tissue mechanically prevents cervical closure and impairs myometrial contraction, leading to ongoing haemorrhage. Removal with sponge forceps or ring forceps can be performed at the bedside and often produces immediate relief. Definitive surgical evacuation should follow to ensure complete uterine emptying. Rh-negative patients require anti-D immunoglobulin within 72 hours of any pregnancy loss event beyond 10 weeks or with surgical intervention.
key_points: Tissue at the cervical os is a mechanical cause of ongoing haemorrhage requiring immediate removal
Cervical shock (bradycardia and hypotension from cervical distension) may occur and resolves with tissue removal
Anti-D immunoglobulin is indicated for Rh-negative women after miscarriage events
Surgical evacuation is preferred over medical management when bleeding is heavy and haemoglobin is falling
explanation.pearls: In Rh-negative women, anti-D immunoglobulin (250 IU before 12 weeks, 625 IU at or after 12 weeks) should be given within 72 hours of miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy, or any uterine instrumentation.

sources.label: RANZCOG - Management of Early Pregnancy Loss

stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with 8 months of amenorrhoea, frequent hot flushes disrupting sleep, and vaginal dryness affecting her quality of life. She has an intact uterus. Her mother had breast cancer diagnosed at age 74. She has no personal history of VTE, cardiovascular disease, or hormone-sensitive malignancy. BMI is 26 kg/m2.

data_table.Investigations: FSH 68 IU/L, oestradiol 42 pmol/L, mammogram (6 months ago) BI-RADS 1

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial hormonal treatment?

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patches alone titrated to symptom control over 3 months
options.rationale: Unopposed oestrogen in a woman with an intact uterus significantly increases the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and endometrial cancer. A progestogen must be added for endometrial protection. Oestrogen-only therapy is reserved for women who have had a hysterectomy. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society - HRT Guideline)

options.text: Oral conjugated equine oestrogens with continuous medroxyprogesterone acetate daily
options.rationale: While combined oral HRT provides both oestrogen and endometrial protection, oral oestrogens carry a higher risk of VTE compared with transdermal delivery due to first-pass hepatic metabolism and prothrombotic effects. Current Australian guidance favours transdermal oestradiol as the preferred route. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society - HRT Guideline)

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patches combined with cyclical oral micronised progesterone
options.rationale: Transdermal oestradiol is preferred over oral oestrogen as it avoids first-pass hepatic metabolism and carries lower VTE risk. In a woman with an intact uterus, a progestogen is mandatory for endometrial protection. Cyclical progesterone (12-14 days per cycle) is appropriate in the perimenopausal transition and early menopause. Micronised progesterone has a favourable metabolic and breast safety profile compared with synthetic progestins. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society - HRT Guideline)

options.text: Oral tibolone 2.5 mg daily as a single-agent combined hormonal preparation
options.rationale: Tibolone is a synthetic steroid with oestrogenic, progestogenic, and androgenic activity that can treat menopausal symptoms and provides endometrial protection. However, it is generally reserved for women who are at least 12 months postmenopausal and is associated with increased stroke risk in older women. It is not typically first-line for initial HRT. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Endocrinology)

options.text: Vaginal oestrogen pessary alone for both vasomotor and urogenital symptoms
options.rationale: Vaginal oestrogen effectively treats urogenital symptoms (dryness, dyspareunia) but has minimal systemic absorption and does not adequately treat vasomotor symptoms such as hot flushes and night sweats. Systemic HRT is needed for moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society - HRT Guideline)

explanation.summary: Menopausal hormone therapy remains the most effective treatment for moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms. In women with an intact uterus, oestrogen must always be combined with a progestogen for endometrial protection. Transdermal oestradiol is preferred over oral preparations due to lower VTE and stroke risk. A first-degree relative with breast cancer diagnosed after age 60 does not contraindicate HRT but warrants informed discussion. The choice between cyclical and continuous progestogen depends on menopausal stage - cyclical is used in early menopause and continuous after 12 months of amenorrhoea or 2 years of cyclical HRT.
key_points: Unopposed oestrogen is contraindicated in women with an intact uterus due to endometrial cancer risk
Transdermal oestradiol carries lower VTE risk than oral oestrogen
Micronised progesterone has a more favourable safety profile than synthetic progestins
A family history of breast cancer diagnosed after age 60 is not an absolute contraindication to HRT
explanation.pearls: Transdermal oestradiol at standard doses (25-50 mcg/day) does not significantly increase VTE risk, unlike oral oestrogens which increase VTE risk 2-fold through hepatic first-pass prothrombotic protein synthesis.

stem: A 28-year-old woman attends for routine cervical screening. She has no symptoms and no history of abnormal cervical pathology. She received the full course of HPV vaccination at age 13. Her last cervical screening test was a negative HPV test 5 years ago. She asks about the recommended screening interval and method.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding her cervical screening?

options.text: She is overdue and should have a clinician-collected HPV test today with repeat screening in 3 years
options.rationale: Under the renewed NCSP, the screening interval is 5 years for HPV-based testing, not 3 years. At 5 years since her last negative HPV test, she is due now, not overdue. The 3-year interval was from the old Pap smear-based program. (Source: Cancer Council Australia - National Cervical Screening Program)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia - National Cervical Screening Program

options.text: She is not yet due for screening and should return at age 30 for her next scheduled HPV test
options.rationale: She is exactly 5 years from her last negative HPV test, so she is due for screening now. There is no age-based deferral to 30 under the current NCSP. Screening commences from age 25 (or 2 years after first sexual activity in unvaccinated individuals) and continues 5-yearly. (Source: Cancer Council Australia - National Cervical Screening Program)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia - National Cervical Screening Program

options.text: She should have conventional cytology given her vaccinated status and repeat screening in 3 years
options.rationale: The renewed NCSP replaced cytology-based screening with primary HPV testing in December 2017. Conventional cytology (Pap smear) is no longer the primary screening modality in Australia. HPV vaccination does not change the screening method, only the expected prevalence of high-risk HPV types. (Source: Cancer Council Australia - National Cervical Screening Program)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia - National Cervical Screening Program

options.text: She is due now and can choose either self-collected vaginal or clinician-collected cervical HPV test
options.rationale: Under the renewed NCSP, she is due at 5 years from her last negative HPV test. Since 1 July 2022, all eligible screening participants in Australia can choose self-collection as an alternative to clinician-collected samples. Both methods use PCR-based HPV testing with equivalent sensitivity for detecting high-risk HPV types. If HPV is detected on self-collection, reflex liquid-based cytology requires a clinician-collected sample. (Source: Cancer Council Australia - National Cervical Screening Program)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia - National Cervical Screening Program

options.text: She can safely extend her screening interval to 10 years given HPV vaccination and a prior negative result
options.rationale: HPV vaccination does not alter the recommended screening interval under the NCSP. The vaccine covers the most common oncogenic HPV types (16 and 18) but does not protect against all high-risk types. The 5-yearly screening interval applies regardless of vaccination status. (Source: Cancer Council Australia - National Cervical Screening Program)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia - National Cervical Screening Program

explanation.summary: Australia's renewed National Cervical Screening Program uses primary HPV testing every 5 years for women aged 25-74, replacing the previous 2-yearly Pap smear program. Since July 2022, self-collection has been available to all eligible participants, improving access and participation rates. If HPV is detected, reflex liquid-based cytology is performed on the same clinician-collected sample or a subsequent clinician-collected sample if the initial test was self-collected. HPV vaccination reduces but does not eliminate cervical cancer risk, and vaccinated individuals follow the same screening schedule.
key_points: NCSP uses primary HPV testing every 5 years from age 25 to 74
Self-collection is available to all eligible participants since July 2022
HPV vaccination does not alter the screening interval or method
Positive HPV results require reflex cytology from a clinician-collected sample
explanation.pearls: Self-collected vaginal HPV samples have sensitivity above 90% for detecting CIN2+ when using PCR-based assays, making them clinically equivalent to clinician-collected samples for primary HPV screening.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia - National Cervical Screening Program
Australian Government - National Cervical Screening Program

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents with irregular menstrual cycles occurring every 45-90 days for the past 3 years, moderate acne, and a BMI of 31 kg/m2. She has no hirsutism. She does not desire pregnancy. Pelvic ultrasound shows bilateral ovaries with 14 and 16 antral follicles respectively and normal ovarian volume.

data_table.Investigations: Total testosterone 2.8 nmol/L (ref 0.5-1.8), SHBG 22 nmol/L (ref 20-130), free androgen index 12.7 (ref < 5), DHEAS 8.2 micromol/L (ref 2.7-9.2), TSH 2.1 mIU/L, prolactin 180 mIU/L (ref < 500), 17-OH progesterone 4.2 nmol/L (ref < 6.0)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Metformin 500 mg twice daily with dose titration over 4 weeks to a target of 2000 mg daily
options.rationale: While metformin has a role in PCOS for metabolic and ovulatory benefits, it is not first-line for menstrual regulation in women not seeking pregnancy. The combined oral contraceptive pill is first-line for cycle regulation, acne management, and endometrial protection when contraception is desired. Metformin may be added as adjunctive therapy. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS - Monash)
source_refs: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS - Monash

options.text: Spironolactone 50 mg twice daily with monthly monitoring of serum potassium and renal function
options.rationale: Spironolactone is an effective anti-androgen for hirsutism and acne in PCOS but requires reliable contraception due to teratogenicity (feminisation of male foetus). It is not first-line monotherapy and is typically added after or alongside the combined oral contraceptive pill. It does not address menstrual irregularity or endometrial protection. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS - Monash)
source_refs: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS - Monash

options.text: Clomifene citrate 50 mg daily on days 2 to 6 with concurrent ultrasound follicle tracking
options.rationale: Clomifene citrate is an ovulation induction agent used when pregnancy is desired. This patient is not seeking pregnancy, so ovulation induction is inappropriate. Clomifene also carries risks of multiple pregnancy and ovarian hyperstimulation that are unnecessary in this context. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS - Monash)
source_refs: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS - Monash

options.text: Cyproterone acetate 100 mg daily for 21 days each cycle as monotherapy for acne and hirsutism
options.rationale: High-dose cyproterone acetate monotherapy is not recommended as first-line due to hepatotoxicity risk and thrombotic potential without consistent cycle regulation. Low-dose cyproterone acetate is available as a component of certain combined oral contraceptive pills, which is a more appropriate vehicle. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Dermatology)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill with ethinylestradiol and cyproterone acetate plus lifestyle modification
options.rationale: The combined oral contraceptive pill is first-line for menstrual regulation, endometrial protection, acne management, and contraception in PCOS when pregnancy is not desired. A formulation containing cyproterone acetate has additional anti-androgenic benefit for acne. Lifestyle modification (structured exercise and dietary counselling) is a cornerstone of PCOS management, particularly with elevated BMI. (Source: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS - Monash)
source_refs: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS - Monash

explanation.summary: PCOS is diagnosed using the Rotterdam criteria when two of three features are present: oligo-ovulation or anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound (12 or more antral follicles per ovary or ovarian volume greater than 10 mL). This patient meets all three criteria. Management depends on whether pregnancy is desired. For women not seeking pregnancy, the combined oral contraceptive pill is first-line for menstrual regulation, endometrial protection, and androgen suppression. Lifestyle modification targeting 5-10% weight loss significantly improves metabolic and reproductive outcomes.
key_points: Rotterdam criteria require 2 of 3: oligo-anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovarian morphology
COCP is first-line for menstrual regulation and endometrial protection when pregnancy is not desired
Anti-androgens such as spironolactone require reliable contraception due to teratogenicity
5-10% weight loss improves ovulatory function, insulin sensitivity, and androgen levels
explanation.pearls: Free androgen index (FAI = total testosterone/SHBG x 100) above 5 is the most sensitive biochemical marker for hyperandrogenism in PCOS, often elevated even when total testosterone is within the normal range.

sources.label: International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS - Monash

stem: A 33-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation is diagnosed with gestational diabetes following a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test. She has no prior history of diabetes. Her BMI at booking was 29 kg/m2. She has been performing self-monitoring of blood glucose levels for 2 weeks following dietary and exercise counselling.

data_table.Investigations: OGTT results - fasting 5.6 mmol/L (ref < 5.1), 1-hour 10.8 mmol/L (ref < 10.0), 2-hour 9.2 mmol/L (ref < 8.5). Home BSL diary (past week) - fasting 5.4-6.1 mmol/L, 1-hour postprandial 7.8-9.4 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg with dinner and titrate to achieve fasting BSL below 5.0 mmol/L
options.rationale: This patient has failed to achieve glycaemic targets after 1-2 weeks of lifestyle modification. The ADIPS targets are fasting BSL below 5.0 mmol/L and 1-hour postprandial below 7.4 mmol/L. Her fasting and postprandial values are consistently above target. Metformin is an appropriate first-line pharmacological agent for GDM in Australia, particularly when fasting hyperglycaemia predominates. (Source: ADIPS - GDM Management Guidelines)

options.text: Continue dietary modification for an additional 2 weeks before reassessing glycaemic control
options.rationale: The standard trial of lifestyle modification for GDM is 1-2 weeks. After 2 weeks with consistently elevated fasting and postprandial glucose, continuing diet alone delays achievement of glycaemic targets and risks adverse foetal outcomes including macrosomia. Pharmacotherapy should be initiated. (Source: ADIPS - GDM Management Guidelines)

options.text: Commence insulin glargine at bedtime starting at 4 units with self-titration protocols
options.rationale: While insulin is effective and safe in GDM, ADIPS guidelines support metformin as an appropriate first-line pharmacological option when lifestyle measures fail. Insulin is preferred when metformin is contraindicated or insufficient, or in certain clinical scenarios such as very high glucose levels. Metformin should be trialled first. (Source: ADIPS - GDM Management Guidelines)

options.text: Commence glibenclamide 2.5 mg with breakfast as a first-line oral hypoglycaemic agent
options.rationale: Glibenclamide (glyburide) crosses the placenta and is associated with higher rates of neonatal hypoglycaemia and macrosomia compared with metformin and insulin. It is not recommended as first-line pharmacotherapy for GDM in current Australian guidelines. (Source: ADIPS - GDM Management Guidelines)

options.text: Refer directly for elective caesarean section at 38 weeks given the elevated glucose levels
options.rationale: GDM alone is not an indication for elective caesarean section. The mode of delivery depends on glycaemic control, estimated foetal weight, and obstetric factors. With appropriate treatment, most women with GDM can aim for vaginal birth. Induction of labour at 38-39 weeks may be considered if control is suboptimal. (Source: RANZCOG - GDM Guideline)

explanation.summary: Gestational diabetes management follows a stepwise approach: lifestyle modification (medical nutrition therapy and structured physical activity) for 1-2 weeks, followed by pharmacotherapy if glycaemic targets are not met. ADIPS targets are fasting BSL below 5.0 mmol/L, 1-hour postprandial below 7.4 mmol/L, and 2-hour postprandial below 6.7 mmol/L. Metformin is an acceptable first-line oral agent when diet fails, with insulin added if metformin alone is insufficient. Ongoing foetal surveillance with serial growth ultrasounds is essential given the risk of macrosomia and related complications.
key_points: ADIPS diagnostic criteria for GDM on 75 g OGTT - fasting 5.1 or above, 1-hour 10.0 or above, 2-hour 8.5 or above mmol/L
ADIPS treatment targets - fasting below 5.0, 1-hour postprandial below 7.4, 2-hour postprandial below 6.7 mmol/L
Lifestyle modification trial of 1-2 weeks before pharmacotherapy is recommended
Metformin is an appropriate first-line pharmacological option when lifestyle measures are insufficient
explanation.pearls: Metformin crosses the placenta but long-term follow-up studies of children exposed to metformin in utero (MiG TOFU trial) show no adverse developmental effects at 2 years, though children may have slightly higher subcutaneous fat.

stem: A 30-year-old Rh-negative woman at 18 weeks gestation presents after a minor motor vehicle accident. She was wearing a seatbelt and reports mild abdominal discomfort but no vaginal bleeding. Foetal movements are present. Her antibody screen at booking was negative. The foetal Rh status is unknown.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 84 bpm, BP 120/76 mmHg, RR 14, Temp 36.7 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Uterus palpable at the umbilicus, non-tender, no uterine contractions on palpation, no vaginal bleeding on speculum

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management regarding Rh prophylaxis?

options.text: No anti-D required as there is no vaginal bleeding and the clinical examination is fully reassuring in this case
options.rationale: Abdominal trauma is a recognised sensitising event regardless of whether vaginal bleeding is present, as fetomaternal haemorrhage can occur without external signs. Anti-D immunoglobulin is indicated after any potential sensitising event in Rh-negative women with no pre-existing anti-D antibodies. (Source: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood - Anti-D Guidelines)
source_refs: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood - Anti-D Guidelines

options.text: Administer anti-D immunoglobulin 625 IU intramuscularly and perform Kleihauer-Betke test for large fetomaternal haemorrhage
options.rationale: At 18 weeks (beyond 12 weeks gestation), the recommended dose of anti-D is 625 IU intramuscularly. A Kleihauer-Betke test (or flow cytometry) should be performed to quantify fetomaternal haemorrhage, as large-volume bleeds after trauma may require additional doses of anti-D. The standard 625 IU dose covers approximately 6 mL of foetal red cells. (Source: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood - Anti-D Guidelines)
source_refs: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood - Anti-D Guidelines

options.text: Administer anti-D immunoglobulin 250 IU intramuscularly and arrange follow-up ultrasound in 48 hours for reassessment
options.rationale: The 250 IU dose of anti-D is appropriate for sensitising events before 12 weeks gestation. At 18 weeks gestation, the dose is 625 IU because the volume of foetal blood in the fetoplacental circulation is larger. Additionally, Kleihauer-Betke testing is needed to detect large fetomaternal haemorrhage. (Source: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood - Anti-D Guidelines)
source_refs: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood - Anti-D Guidelines

options.text: Defer anti-D until the routine 28-week prophylaxis dose as there is no evidence of active vaginal bleeding currently
options.rationale: Routine antenatal prophylaxis at 28 weeks does not replace the need for event-based anti-D administration. Abdominal trauma is a sensitising event requiring prompt anti-D regardless of scheduled prophylaxis timing. Waiting until 28 weeks risks sensitisation from a fetomaternal haemorrhage occurring now. (Source: RANZCOG - Guidelines on the Use of Anti-D)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Guidelines on the Use of Anti-D

options.text: Perform Kleihauer-Betke test first and administer anti-D only if the result confirms significant fetomaternal haemorrhage
options.rationale: Anti-D should be administered as soon as possible after the sensitising event (ideally within 72 hours) and should not be withheld pending Kleihauer-Betke results. The Kleihauer test determines whether additional anti-D is needed beyond the standard dose, not whether anti-D should be given at all. (Source: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood - Anti-D Guidelines)
source_refs: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood - Anti-D Guidelines

explanation.summary: Rh-negative women require anti-D immunoglobulin after any potential sensitising event to prevent Rh isoimmunisation. Sensitising events include abdominal trauma, vaginal bleeding, invasive procedures, miscarriage, and ectopic pregnancy. The dose depends on gestational age: 250 IU before 12 weeks, 625 IU at or after 12 weeks. Kleihauer-Betke testing or flow cytometry is performed after events at or beyond 12 weeks to detect large fetomaternal haemorrhage requiring additional anti-D. Anti-D should be given within 72 hours of the event.
key_points: Abdominal trauma is a sensitising event requiring anti-D regardless of vaginal bleeding
Anti-D dose is 250 IU before 12 weeks and 625 IU at or after 12 weeks gestation
Kleihauer-Betke test quantifies fetomaternal haemorrhage to determine if additional anti-D is needed
Standard 625 IU dose covers approximately 6 mL of foetal red cells
explanation.pearls: Anti-D immunoglobulin should be given within 72 hours of a sensitising event, but if missed, administration up to 10 days later may still provide some protection and should not be withheld.

sources.label: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood - Anti-D Guidelines
RANZCOG - Guidelines on the Use of Anti-D

stem: A 34-year-old woman at 33 weeks gestation presents with intense generalised pruritus for 5 days that is worse on her palms and soles at night. She has no rash, no abdominal pain, and no jaundice. She has had an otherwise uncomplicated pregnancy.

data_table.Investigations: Total bile acids 48 micromol/L (ref < 6), ALT 72 U/L (ref < 35), AST 58 U/L (ref < 35), GGT 28 U/L (ref < 35), bilirubin 14 micromol/L (ref < 20), INR 1.0, hepatitis B and C serology negative, liver ultrasound normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Reassurance that pruritus in pregnancy is common and prescribe emollients with chlorpheniramine at night
options.rationale: Pruritus gravidarum (benign pruritus of pregnancy) is a diagnosis of exclusion. Elevated bile acids above 14 micromol/L confirm intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP), which carries significant foetal risks including stillbirth. Simple emollients and antihistamines are insufficient. (Source: RANZCOG - Obstetric Cholestasis)

options.text: Commence prednisolone 30 mg daily for presumed polymorphic eruption of pregnancy and review in 1 week
options.rationale: Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy (PEP) typically presents with urticarial papules and plaques within striae, not isolated palmoplantar pruritus without rash. The elevated bile acids and transaminases confirm ICP. Systemic corticosteroids are not indicated for ICP. (Source: RANZCOG - Obstetric Cholestasis)

options.text: Commence ursodeoxycholic acid 500 mg twice daily, weekly bile acid and liver function monitoring, and plan delivery at 37 weeks
options.rationale: ICP with bile acids between 40 and 99 micromol/L carries increased risk of adverse perinatal outcomes. Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) is the first-line treatment for symptom relief and may reduce bile acid levels. Weekly monitoring of bile acids and liver function is recommended. Current Australian practice recommends planned delivery at 37 weeks for bile acids 40-99 micromol/L to reduce stillbirth risk. (Source: RANZCOG - Obstetric Cholestasis)

options.text: Commence cholestyramine 4 g four times daily and plan delivery at 39 weeks if symptoms persist on treatment
options.rationale: Cholestyramine is a bile acid sequestrant that may provide symptom relief but impairs absorption of fat-soluble vitamins including vitamin K, potentially worsening coagulopathy risk. It is less effective than UDCA and not first-line. Additionally, delivery at 39 weeks is too late for bile acids of 48 micromol/L - delivery at 37 weeks is recommended. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Gastroenterology)

options.text: Commence ursodeoxycholic acid 250 mg twice daily, fortnightly bile acid monitoring, and plan delivery at 38 weeks
options.rationale: The UDCA dose is subtherapeutic at 500 mg daily for moderate ICP - the recommended dose is 10-15 mg/kg/day, typically 1000-1500 mg daily in divided doses. Fortnightly monitoring is insufficient for bile acids in the 40-99 range, and delivery should be planned at 37 weeks, not 38 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG - Obstetric Cholestasis)

explanation.summary: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy is diagnosed by characteristic pruritus (often palmoplantar, worse at night) combined with elevated serum bile acids above 19 micromol/L. It carries risk of sudden foetal death, the mechanism of which likely involves bile acid-mediated cardiac arrhythmia in the foetus. Risk stratification by bile acid level guides management: mild (19-39 micromol/L) with delivery at 38 weeks, moderate (40-99 micromol/L) with delivery at 37 weeks, and severe (100 micromol/L or above) with individualised earlier delivery. Ursodeoxycholic acid remains first-line treatment despite the PITCHES trial showing limited effect on perinatal outcomes.
key_points: Bile acids above 19 micromol/L with pruritus confirm ICP diagnosis
Risk of stillbirth increases significantly with bile acids above 40 micromol/L
UDCA 10-15 mg/kg/day is first-line treatment for symptom relief
Delivery timing is stratified by bile acid level - 37 weeks for levels 40-99 micromol/L
explanation.pearls: Bile acid levels above 100 micromol/L carry a stillbirth risk of approximately 3.4% - these patients may require inpatient monitoring with CTG and consideration of delivery as early as 34-36 weeks depending on trajectory.

stem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 10 weeks gestation presents with persistent nausea and vomiting for 3 weeks. She has been unable to tolerate any oral intake for the past 48 hours and has lost 4 kg since her booking visit 3 weeks ago. She reports dark concentrated urine and dizziness on standing. She has tried oral ginger and small frequent meals without improvement.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 104 bpm, BP 95/62 mmHg (supine), BP 80/54 mmHg (standing), Temp 37.0 degrees C, weight 58 kg (booking 62 kg)
data_table.Investigations: Urine ketones 3+, sodium 134 mmol/L, potassium 3.1 mmol/L, chloride 92 mmol/L, bicarbonate 30 mmol/L, urea 8.2 mmol/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, TSH 0.08 mIU/L, free T4 24 pmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Oral ondansetron 4 mg wafer and trial of oral fluids before considering admission to hospital
options.rationale: This patient has clinical signs of significant dehydration (postural hypotension, ketonuria) and has been unable to tolerate any oral intake for 48 hours. Attempting oral therapy is inadequate - she requires intravenous fluid resuscitation and parenteral antiemetics. (Source: RANZCOG - Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

options.text: Commence dexamethasone 8 mg IV twice daily as rescue antiemetic therapy for refractory symptoms
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are reserved for refractory hyperemesis that has not responded to first-line and second-line antiemetics. They should be avoided before 10 weeks gestation due to association with orofacial clefts. While this patient is at 10 weeks, corticosteroids should only be considered after failure of standard antiemetics, IV fluids, and electrolyte correction. (Source: RANZCOG - Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

options.text: Commence IV thiamine 100 mg, initiate outpatient IV fluid infusions 3 times weekly, and prescribe metoclopramide
options.rationale: Thiamine supplementation is important to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy, but this patient's degree of dehydration (postural hypotension, 48 hours nil orally, significant ketonuria) and electrolyte derangement (hypokalaemia, metabolic alkalosis) require inpatient management, not outpatient infusions. (Source: Queensland Health - Hyperemesis Gravidarum)

options.text: Admit for IV normal saline with potassium replacement, IV thiamine 100 mg, and IV ondansetron 4 mg 8-hourly
options.rationale: This patient meets criteria for admission: significant dehydration with postural hypotension, inability to tolerate oral intake for 48 hours, greater than 5% weight loss, ketonuria 3+, and electrolyte derangement. Management priorities are IV fluid resuscitation with potassium correction, thiamine supplementation before dextrose-containing fluids to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy, and parenteral antiemetic therapy. The suppressed TSH and elevated free T4 represent gestational thyrotoxicosis from hCG cross-reactivity, which resolves with treatment of hyperemesis. (Source: RANZCOG - Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

options.text: Start carbimazole 10 mg daily for thyrotoxicosis causing the vomiting and arrange thyroid recheck in 2 weeks
options.rationale: The suppressed TSH and mildly elevated free T4 in this context represent gestational thyrotoxicosis caused by hCG cross-reactivity with the TSH receptor. This is a self-limiting condition that resolves as hCG levels decline in the second trimester. Antithyroid drugs are not indicated and could cause hypothyroidism. True hyperthyroidism should be suspected only if TSH receptor antibodies are positive or there are features of Graves disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum is defined by persistent vomiting in pregnancy associated with weight loss exceeding 5%, dehydration, and ketonuria. Admission criteria include inability to tolerate oral intake, significant dehydration or electrolyte derangement, and weight loss exceeding 5% of pre-pregnancy weight. Thiamine must be given before any dextrose-containing IV fluids to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. Gestational thyrotoxicosis from hCG cross-reactivity occurs in up to 60% of hyperemesis cases and does not require antithyroid treatment. The metabolic alkalosis with hypokalaemia and hypochloraemia results from loss of gastric hydrochloric acid.
key_points: Thiamine 100 mg IV must precede dextrose-containing fluids to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
Gestational thyrotoxicosis from hCG cross-reactivity resolves spontaneously and does not require antithyroid drugs
Hypokalaemic hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis is the typical electrolyte pattern in hyperemesis
Admission is indicated for weight loss above 5%, inability to tolerate oral intake, or electrolyte derangement
explanation.pearls: Wernicke encephalopathy from hyperemesis gravidarum can present atypically without the classic triad - any patient with prolonged vomiting in pregnancy should receive IV thiamine 100 mg before glucose-containing fluids.

sources.label: RANZCOG - Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

stem: A 38-year-old woman underwent suction curettage 2 weeks ago for a complete hydatidiform mole confirmed on histopathology. Her pre-evacuation beta-hCG was 285,000 IU/L. She is clinically well with no vaginal bleeding. She asks about follow-up requirements and when she can try to conceive again.

data_table.Investigations: Current beta-hCG 18,400 IU/L, chest X-ray normal, FBC and UEC normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding post-molar follow-up?

options.text: Serial weekly beta-hCG until normal, then 6 monthly levels for 1 year before any conception attempt is allowed
options.rationale: The monitoring interval after normalisation is too long and the total surveillance duration is not aligned with Australian GTD registry protocols. For a complete mole, once beta-hCG normalises, monthly monitoring is required, and the total surveillance period after normalisation depends on whether the decline was spontaneous. (Source: ANZGOG - GTD Management Guidelines)

options.text: No further monitoring needed as beta-hCG is declining appropriately and she can conceive at any time now
options.rationale: A declining beta-hCG does not exclude subsequent malignant transformation to gestational trophoblastic neoplasia (GTN). Complete moles have approximately 15-20% risk of developing GTN. Serial monitoring to confirm normalisation and maintained normalisation is mandatory. (Source: ANZGOG - GTD Management Guidelines)

options.text: Register with the Australian GTD registry, commence chemotherapy now given the high initial beta-hCG level
options.rationale: Registration with the GTD registry is appropriate, but chemotherapy is not indicated with a declining beta-hCG. Chemotherapy for GTN is indicated when beta-hCG plateaus (less than 10% decline over 3 consecutive weekly values), rises, or remains elevated beyond 6 months post-evacuation. This patient's level is declining appropriately. (Source: ANZGOG - GTD Management Guidelines)

options.text: Commence combined oral contraceptive pill immediately and repeat curettage to ensure complete uterine evacuation
options.rationale: Repeat curettage is not routinely indicated after molar evacuation unless there is significant retained tissue causing haemorrhage, and carries risk of uterine perforation and Asherman syndrome. The COCP is recommended during the surveillance period for contraception, but a second curettage is not appropriate here with beta-hCG declining. (Source: ANZGOG - GTD Management Guidelines)

options.text: Register with the GTD registry, serial weekly beta-hCG until normal, then monthly levels for 6 months with reliable contraception
options.rationale: Complete mole management requires registration with the Australian and New Zealand GTD registry for centralised monitoring. Weekly beta-hCG is measured until normalisation, then monthly levels for 6 months post-normalisation for complete moles (12 months for partial moles). Reliable contraception is essential throughout surveillance as pregnancy would confound beta-hCG monitoring and could mask GTN. Conception can be attempted after completing the surveillance period. (Source: ANZGOG - GTD Management Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Gestational trophoblastic disease requires structured post-evacuation surveillance through the Australian and New Zealand GTD registry. Complete hydatidiform moles carry a 15-20% risk of malignant transformation to gestational trophoblastic neoplasia. Serial weekly beta-hCG levels are monitored until normalisation, followed by monthly levels for 6 months for complete moles. Reliable contraception is mandatory throughout surveillance to avoid pregnancy confounding beta-hCG interpretation. Indications for chemotherapy include plateauing or rising beta-hCG, histological evidence of choriocarcinoma, or metastatic disease.
key_points: Complete moles carry 15-20% risk of developing GTN requiring chemotherapy
Weekly beta-hCG monitoring until normalisation, then monthly for 6 months post-normalisation
Reliable contraception is essential throughout the surveillance period
Rising or plateauing beta-hCG mandates further investigation and likely chemotherapy
explanation.pearls: The surveillance period after partial mole is 12 months post-normalisation of beta-hCG (compared with 6 months for complete mole), reflecting the lower but not negligible risk of malignant transformation (approximately 0.5-5%).

stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of vague pelvic fullness. Transvaginal ultrasound identifies a 7 cm left ovarian cyst that is unilocular with thin smooth walls, no solid components, no septae, and no internal vascularity on Doppler. The right ovary appears normal. She is premenopausal with regular cycles.

data_table.Investigations: CA-125 18 U/mL (ref < 35), beta-hCG negative, FBC normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat transvaginal ultrasound in 8-12 weeks to assess for spontaneous resolution of the cyst
options.rationale: Simple ovarian cysts up to 7 cm in premenopausal women are almost always benign (functional cysts). International Ovarian Tumour Analysis (IOTA) simple rules classify this as a benign pattern (unilocular, smooth walls, no solid components, no Doppler flow). For cysts 5-7 cm, repeat imaging in 8-12 weeks is appropriate to confirm resolution, as most functional cysts resolve spontaneously within 2-3 cycles. CA-125 is normal, further supporting benign aetiology. (Source: RANZCOG - Ovarian Cysts)

options.text: Urgent referral to gynaecological oncology for suspected ovarian malignancy based on size
options.rationale: This cyst has all features of a simple benign cyst: unilocular, thin-walled, no solid components, no septae, no Doppler flow, and a normal CA-125. Gynaecological oncology referral is indicated for complex cysts with features suggesting malignancy (solid components, papillary projections, irregular septae, ascites, elevated CA-125). (Source: RANZCOG - Ovarian Cysts)

options.text: Laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy to obtain histological diagnosis and prevent ovarian torsion
options.rationale: Surgical intervention is not indicated for a simple cyst that may resolve spontaneously. Surgery carries risks of reduced ovarian reserve, adhesions, and anaesthetic complications. Torsion risk is present but does not mandate prophylactic surgery for a simple cyst. Surgery is considered if the cyst persists beyond 2-3 cycles or is enlarging. (Source: RANZCOG - Ovarian Cysts)

options.text: Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill to promote cyst resolution over the next 6 weeks
options.rationale: The combined oral contraceptive pill does not accelerate resolution of existing ovarian cysts, although it may reduce the formation of new functional cysts. A Cochrane review found no benefit of oral contraceptives for expectant management of functional ovarian cysts. (Source: Cochrane - Oral Contraceptives for Functional Ovarian Cysts)
source_refs: Cochrane - Oral Contraceptives for Functional Ovarian Cysts

options.text: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast to better characterise the cyst before further management
options.rationale: CT is inferior to transvaginal ultrasound for ovarian cyst characterisation and adds unnecessary radiation exposure. Ultrasound is the imaging modality of choice for assessing ovarian cysts. MRI may be considered for indeterminate lesions on ultrasound, but CT is not the appropriate next step. (Source: RANZCOG - Ovarian Cysts)

explanation.summary: Simple ovarian cysts in premenopausal women are overwhelmingly benign, most commonly functional (follicular or corpus luteum) cysts. The IOTA simple rules and Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI) help stratify risk. Features suggesting malignancy include solid components, papillary projections, thick irregular septae, ascites, bilateral involvement, and elevated CA-125. Simple unilocular cysts up to 7 cm can be managed expectantly with repeat imaging. Surgery is indicated for persistent cysts, complex features, enlarging cysts, or symptomatic torsion. In postmenopausal women, the threshold for intervention is lower as functional cysts are no longer expected.
key_points: Simple unilocular ovarian cysts up to 7 cm in premenopausal women are almost always benign
IOTA simple rules help classify benign versus malignant features on ultrasound
Oral contraceptives do not accelerate resolution of existing functional cysts
Postmenopausal women with ovarian cysts require more aggressive investigation due to higher malignancy risk
explanation.pearls: CA-125 has poor specificity in premenopausal women as it is elevated in endometriosis, fibroids, PID, and during menstruation - it is most useful in postmenopausal women where the Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI = ultrasound score x menopausal status x CA-125) above 200 warrants gynaecological oncology referral.

sources.label: Cochrane - Oral Contraceptives for Functional Ovarian Cysts
RCOG - Management of Suspected Ovarian Masses in Premenopausal Women

stem: A 31-year-old woman presents at 7 weeks gestation with right-sided pelvic pain and light vaginal bleeding. She had a right salpingectomy 2 years ago for a tubal ectopic pregnancy. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a gestational sac with a yolk sac located eccentrically in the right cornual region of the uterus, surrounded by less than 5 mm of myometrium. The interstitial line sign is positive. Beta-hCG is 12,800 IU/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 82 bpm, BP 122/74 mmHg, Temp 36.8 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Mild right adnexal tenderness, no peritonism, no cervical motion tenderness

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 as for standard tubal ectopic protocol
options.rationale: While methotrexate can be used for interstitial ectopic pregnancy, single-dose methotrexate has significantly lower success rates for interstitial ectopics (50-65%) compared with tubal ectopics. The higher beta-hCG level and vascularity of the interstitial segment increase rupture risk. Multi-dose protocols or direct injection may be considered, but many centres prefer surgical management. (Source: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

options.text: Laparoscopic cornuostomy or cornual wedge resection by a surgeon experienced in this procedure
options.rationale: Interstitial (cornual) ectopic pregnancy is a high-risk variant accounting for 2-4% of ectopic pregnancies, with mortality 2-2.5 times higher than tubal ectopics due to the rich myometrial blood supply and potential for catastrophic haemorrhage at rupture. With a beta-hCG of 12,800, visible gestational sac with yolk sac, and thin overlying myometrium (less than 5 mm), surgical management by laparoscopic cornuostomy or cornual wedge resection by an experienced operator is the safest approach. (Source: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

options.text: Expectant management with 48-hourly beta-hCG levels as the patient is currently haemodynamically stable
options.rationale: Expectant management is inappropriate for interstitial ectopic pregnancy with a rising or high beta-hCG and a visible gestational sac. The interstitial segment can expand to accommodate the pregnancy before catastrophic rupture at 8-16 weeks, with massive intraperitoneal haemorrhage. Active intervention is required. (Source: KEMH Clinical Guidelines - Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Emergency laparotomy with ipsilateral cornual resection and possible hysterectomy for definitive management
options.rationale: Emergency laparotomy is indicated for ruptured interstitial ectopic with haemodynamic instability. This patient is currently stable with no signs of rupture. A planned laparoscopic approach by an experienced surgeon is appropriate and avoids the morbidity of laparotomy. Hysterectomy is rarely necessary when resection is performed before rupture. (Source: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

options.text: Ultrasound-guided local injection of methotrexate directly into the gestational sac under procedural sedation
options.rationale: Local methotrexate injection is a technique used at some centres for interstitial ectopic but requires interventional radiology expertise and is not widely available. When available, it can be effective but carries risk of incomplete treatment. Laparoscopic surgical management remains the standard approach when the diagnosis is clear. (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Ectopic Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Interstitial ectopic pregnancy implants within the intramural portion of the fallopian tube where it traverses the myometrium. It is particularly dangerous because the thick myometrial wall allows the pregnancy to grow larger before rupture, leading to massive haemorrhage from the uterine and ovarian vessels. The interstitial line sign on ultrasound (an echogenic line extending from the endometrial cavity to the ectopic mass) helps distinguish it from an angular pregnancy. Previous ipsilateral salpingectomy is a risk factor. Surgical management by an experienced operator is preferred for most cases, particularly with higher beta-hCG levels and visible embryonic structures.
key_points: Interstitial ectopic accounts for 2-4% of ectopics but has 2-2.5 times higher mortality than tubal ectopics
The interstitial line sign is a key ultrasound diagnostic feature
Previous ipsilateral salpingectomy is a risk factor for interstitial ectopic
Laparoscopic cornuostomy or cornual wedge resection is the surgical approach of choice
explanation.pearls: The interstitial portion of the tube can expand to 3-5 cm before rupture, which may not occur until 8-16 weeks gestation - later than typical tubal ectopics, making high clinical suspicion essential in women with prior salpingectomy.

sources.label: RANZCOG - Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

stem: A 36-year-old woman at 32 weeks gestation in her fourth pregnancy has a known anterior placenta praevia overlying her previous lower segment caesarean section scar from 2 prior caesarean deliveries. Ultrasound at 28 weeks showed loss of the retroplacental clear zone, irregular lacunae within the placenta, and bridging vessels from the placenta into the bladder wall on colour Doppler. MRI has confirmed suspected placenta percreta with likely bladder involvement.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 76 bpm, BP 118/70 mmHg, Temp 36.8 degrees C
data_table.Investigations: Hb 116 g/L, group and hold current, no active bleeding

lead_in: What is the most appropriate delivery plan?

options.text: Attempt vaginal delivery at 39 weeks with uterotonic agents on standby in case of postpartum haemorrhage from the praevia
options.rationale: Vaginal delivery is absolutely contraindicated with placenta praevia. Furthermore, placenta percreta with bladder involvement requires multidisciplinary planned caesarean delivery. Attempting vaginal delivery risks catastrophic haemorrhage and maternal death. (Source: RANZCOG - Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Elective caesarean section at 39 weeks with attempted manual removal of the placenta at the time of delivery
options.rationale: Attempting manual removal of an accreta spectrum placenta is the single most dangerous action, as it disrupts the abnormal placental-myometrial-bladder interface and causes massive uncontrolled haemorrhage. The placenta must be left in situ. Additionally, delivery at 39 weeks is too late for confirmed percreta. (Source: RANZCOG - Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Planned caesarean hysterectomy at 35-36 weeks at a tertiary centre with multidisciplinary team including urology and radiology
options.rationale: Confirmed placenta percreta with bladder involvement requires planned caesarean hysterectomy at a tertiary centre with a multidisciplinary team. Delivery is typically planned at 35-36 weeks to balance prematurity risk against the risk of emergency haemorrhage from unplanned labour or bleeding. The team should include senior obstetric surgeon, urologist (for bladder involvement), interventional radiologist (for potential internal iliac artery balloon occlusion), anaesthetist, neonatologist, and cell salvage/massive transfusion protocol. The placenta is left in situ and removed en bloc with the uterus. (Source: RANZCOG - Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Elective caesarean at 37 weeks with placenta left in situ and methotrexate given postoperatively for placental resorption
options.rationale: Conservative management (leaving the placenta in situ without hysterectomy) may be considered in accreta or increta where the patient strongly desires uterine preservation, but it is not appropriate for percreta with bladder involvement. The risk of secondary haemorrhage, sepsis, and delayed hysterectomy is very high. Methotrexate has not been shown to reliably promote placental resorption and risks immunosuppression. (Source: RANZCOG - Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Admit now for uterine artery embolisation to devascularise the placental bed before planned delivery at 38 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Pre-delivery uterine artery embolisation is not standard practice for placenta accreta spectrum. Interventional radiology may place internal iliac artery balloon catheters preoperatively to assist with haemostasis during caesarean hysterectomy, but embolisation prior to delivery risks placental ischaemia and foetal compromise. (Source: RANZCOG - Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

explanation.summary: Placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) disorders encompass accreta (adheres to myometrium), increta (invades myometrium), and percreta (penetrates through myometrium into adjacent organs). Risk factors include previous caesarean section (strongest risk factor - exponentially increasing with number of prior caesareans), placenta praevia overlying a previous scar, and prior uterine surgery. Planned caesarean hysterectomy at a tertiary centre at 35-36 weeks is the standard of care for percreta. Attempting placental removal is the most dangerous action and must be avoided. The multidisciplinary team approach has significantly reduced maternal mortality from this condition.
key_points: Never attempt manual removal of an accreta spectrum placenta - leave in situ and proceed to hysterectomy
Delivery at 35-36 weeks balances prematurity risk against emergency haemorrhage risk
Multidisciplinary team is essential - obstetrics, urology, interventional radiology, anaesthesia, haematology
The risk of PAS increases exponentially with number of prior caesarean deliveries
explanation.pearls: After 3 prior caesarean deliveries with a concurrent anterior placenta praevia, the risk of placenta accreta spectrum exceeds 60% - this combination should trigger early referral to a PAS-experienced tertiary centre.

sources.label: FIGO - Placenta Accreta Diagnosis and Management
RCOG - Placenta Praevia and Accreta Green-top Guideline

stem: You are the registrar called to a delivery where the midwife reports the foetal head has delivered but the anterior shoulder has not followed despite gentle downward traction. The turtle sign is present with the foetal head retracting tightly against the perineum. McRoberts positioning with suprapubic pressure has been applied for 30 seconds without success. The estimated foetal weight was 4.2 kg on recent ultrasound.

data_table.Examination: Foetal head delivered in OA position, anterior shoulder impacted behind symphysis, turtle sign present, McRoberts with suprapubic pressure unsuccessful

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Apply fundal pressure to help push the anterior shoulder past the symphysis pubis and deliver the baby distally
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated in shoulder dystocia as it further impacts the anterior shoulder behind the symphysis and significantly increases the risk of brachial plexus injury and uterine rupture. This is a critical safety point - fundal pressure worsens the impaction. (Source: RANZCOG - Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Apply strong downward traction on the foetal head to deliver the impacted anterior shoulder beyond the symphysis
options.rationale: Excessive traction on the foetal head is the primary cause of brachial plexus injury in shoulder dystocia. Lateral traction stretches the brachial plexus over the impacted shoulder. Only gentle axial traction should be used, and further pulling when the shoulder is impacted will not resolve the dystocia. (Source: RANZCOG - Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Perform an episiotomy to create more space for the anterior shoulder to pass through the soft tissue outlet
options.rationale: An episiotomy alone does not resolve shoulder dystocia because the obstruction is bony (shoulder against symphysis), not soft tissue. An episiotomy may be performed to facilitate internal manoeuvres (posterior arm delivery, rotational manoeuvres) but is not itself a treatment for the bony impaction. (Source: RANZCOG - Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Perform internal rotational manoeuvres by applying pressure to the posterior shoulder to rotate into an oblique diameter
options.rationale: After McRoberts with suprapubic pressure has failed, internal manoeuvres are the next step. The Rubin II manoeuvre involves reaching behind the posterior shoulder and rotating the foetal shoulders into an oblique diameter, disimpacting the anterior shoulder from behind the symphysis. Wood's screw manoeuvre (pressure on the anterior surface of the posterior shoulder) can also rotate the baby. If rotation fails, delivery of the posterior arm should be attempted. These internal manoeuvres have high success rates. (Source: RANZCOG - Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Move the patient onto all fours (Gaskin manoeuvre) as the immediate next intervention before any internal manoeuvres
options.rationale: The Gaskin (all-fours) manoeuvre can be effective by changing the pelvic dimensions and using gravity to disimpact the posterior shoulder. However, in the HELPERR mnemonic sequence, internal manoeuvres (rotation and posterior arm delivery) are attempted before changing position to all fours when McRoberts and suprapubic pressure have failed. All-fours may be attempted if internal manoeuvres fail. (Source: RANZCOG - Shoulder Dystocia)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is an obstetric emergency managed using the HELPERR mnemonic: Help (call for assistance), Episiotomy (if needed for access), Legs (McRoberts positioning), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter (internal rotational manoeuvres), Remove posterior arm, Roll (Gaskin manoeuvre). After McRoberts and suprapubic pressure fail, the critical next step is internal manoeuvres - either rotational manoeuvres (Rubin II, Wood's screw) or delivery of the posterior arm. Fundal pressure and excessive lateral traction are absolutely contraindicated. The entire sequence should ideally be completed within 5 minutes of head delivery to minimise foetal hypoxia.
key_points: Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated - it worsens impaction and causes injury
McRoberts with suprapubic pressure resolves approximately 50% of shoulder dystocia cases
Internal rotational manoeuvres or posterior arm delivery follow if McRoberts fails
Excessive lateral traction on the foetal head is the primary cause of brachial plexus injury
explanation.pearls: Delivery of the posterior arm has the highest single-manoeuvre success rate (approximately 85%) in shoulder dystocia but is technically demanding - the operator reaches along the posterior arm, flexes the elbow, sweeps the forearm across the chest, and delivers the hand and arm.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar on call. A midwife calls urgently from the birth suite. A 28-year-old woman at 37 weeks gestation with a singleton pregnancy had spontaneous rupture of membranes 5 minutes ago. On vaginal examination, the midwife palpated a pulsating cord loop below the presenting part. The foetal head is at minus 2 station. The CTG shows acute foetal bradycardia at 80 bpm. Cervical dilatation is 5 cm.

data_table.Examination: Cervix 5 cm dilated, cephalic at -2 station, pulsating cord below presenting part, membranes ruptured, liquor clear

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord, fill the bladder with 500 mL warm saline, and expedite category 1 caesarean section
options.rationale: Manual elevation of the presenting part is correct as the first action and bladder filling to maintain elevation is an appropriate adjunct. However, the critical missing step is positioning the patient in the knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position, which is the most immediately effective action to relieve cord compression alongside manual elevation. (Source: RANZCOG - Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Commence IV oxytocin augmentation to expedite vaginal delivery as the cervix is already 5 cm dilated with active labour
options.rationale: At 5 cm dilatation with the presenting part at minus 2 station, vaginal delivery is not imminent and cannot be expedited safely in the timeframe required to prevent foetal hypoxic brain injury. Cord prolapse with sustained bradycardia requires delivery within 30 minutes. Emergency caesarean section is the appropriate mode of delivery. (Source: RANZCOG - Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Attempt to replace the cord above the presenting part and apply a foetal scalp electrode for continuous heart rate monitoring
options.rationale: Cord replacement (funic reduction) is not recommended as it is rarely successful, risks cord spasm and vessel damage, and delays definitive management. The cord should be kept warm and moist but not handled excessively. The focus must be on relieving cord compression and expediting delivery. (Source: RANZCOG - Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 mcg subcutaneously to stop contractions and wait for the cord pulsation to improve and normalise
options.rationale: Tocolysis with terbutaline may be considered as an adjunct to reduce uterine contractions contributing to cord compression while preparing for emergency caesarean section. However, it is not a definitive treatment and must not delay surgical delivery. Waiting for the CTG to normalise before proceeding would unacceptably delay delivery. (Source: RANZCOG - Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Place the patient in steep Trendelenburg or knee-chest position, manually elevate the presenting part, and call for category 1 caesarean
options.rationale: The immediate priorities in cord prolapse are to relieve compression on the cord and expedite delivery. Positioning in steep Trendelenburg or knee-chest uses gravity to lift the presenting part off the cord. Continuous manual elevation of the presenting part via vaginal examination maintains this relief. Category 1 (emergency) caesarean section is called with a target decision-to-delivery interval of under 30 minutes. Retrograde bladder filling with 500-750 mL saline can maintain elevation during transfer to theatre. The cord should be kept warm and moist, and minimal handling is important to prevent vasospasm. (Source: RANZCOG - Cord Prolapse)

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency with a risk of foetal hypoxia and death if not managed rapidly. The immediate management triad is: position (steep Trendelenburg or knee-chest), elevate (manually lift the presenting part off the cord), and deliver (emergency caesarean section unless vaginal delivery is imminent). Retrograde bladder filling with 500-750 mL warm saline via Foley catheter can maintain upward displacement of the presenting part during transfer to theatre. Risk factors include malpresentation, polyhydramnios, preterm gestation, artificial rupture of membranes with a high presenting part, and multiparity.
key_points: Knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg positioning is the most immediately effective action to relieve cord compression
Manual elevation of the presenting part must be maintained continuously until delivery
Category 1 caesarean section with decision-to-delivery time under 30 minutes is the target
Retrograde bladder filling with 500-750 mL saline can maintain presenting part elevation during transfer
explanation.pearls: Cord prolapse occurs most commonly with artificial rupture of membranes when the presenting part is not well applied to the cervix - always confirm the presenting part is well engaged before amniotomy, and never perform amniotomy with a high mobile head.

sources.label: RCOG - Umbilical Cord Prolapse Green-top Guideline

stem: A 35-year-old woman is 10 minutes post-delivery of a term infant via normal vaginal birth after induction of labour with oxytocin. She suddenly becomes acutely dyspnoeic, agitated, and cyanotic. She rapidly loses consciousness and develops generalised tonic-clonic convulsions. The midwife notices profuse vaginal bleeding that does not respond to fundal massage and oxytocin.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130 bpm, BP 70/40 mmHg, SpO2 78% on 15 L non-rebreather mask, RR 32, Temp 37.2 degrees C
data_table.Investigations: Point-of-care blood gas - pH 7.18, lactate 8.2 mmol/L, fibrinogen 0.6 g/L (ref 2.0-4.0)

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate initial management priority?

options.text: Amniotic fluid embolism - activate massive transfusion protocol, commence aggressive resuscitation with high-flow oxygen, and prioritise early fibrinogen replacement
options.rationale: This presentation is classic amniotic fluid embolism (AFE): sudden cardiovascular collapse, acute hypoxia, seizures, and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) with coagulopathic haemorrhage shortly after delivery. The critically low fibrinogen of 0.6 g/L confirms consumptive coagulopathy. Management is supportive: aggressive resuscitation, massive transfusion protocol activation, and early replacement of fibrinogen (target above 2.0 g/L) with cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate. (Source: ANZCA - Obstetric Anaesthesia Guidelines)

options.text: Massive pulmonary embolism - commence therapeutic heparin infusion and arrange CT pulmonary angiography while stabilising with vasopressors
options.rationale: While acute PE can cause sudden cardiovascular collapse and hypoxia, the combination of seizures and immediate-onset DIC with coagulopathic haemorrhage is not typical of PE. Furthermore, commencing anticoagulation in the context of active postpartum haemorrhage and DIC would be catastrophic. The temporal relationship to delivery and the coagulopathy pattern point to AFE. (Source: RANZCOG - Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

options.text: Eclamptic seizure with uterine atony - administer magnesium sulfate 4 g IV loading dose with bimanual uterine compression and uterotonics
options.rationale: Eclampsia typically presents with hypertension and proteinuria, and postpartum eclampsia is usually not associated with acute cardiovascular collapse, cyanosis, or DIC with hypofibrinogenaemia. This patient's BP is profoundly low, not elevated. The degree of hypoxia and coagulopathy is not explained by eclampsia alone. (Source: RANZCOG - Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

options.text: Uterine atony with air embolism - position in left lateral with head down, commence IV oxytocin infusion, and aspirate air via central venous catheter
options.rationale: Air embolism is extremely rare in vaginal delivery and typically occurs during caesarean section or uterine manipulation. There are no features here suggesting air entry. The seizures, acute DIC, and pattern of collapse are characteristic of AFE rather than air embolism. (Source: RANZCOG - Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

options.text: Septic shock from chorioamnionitis - commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics with aggressive fluid resuscitation and vasopressor support
options.rationale: Septic shock from chorioamnionitis develops gradually with fever and tachycardia, not with sudden cardiovascular collapse seconds to minutes after delivery. The acute onset of hypoxia, seizures, and immediate DIC is not consistent with sepsis. The normal temperature further argues against this diagnosis. (Source: ANZCA - Obstetric Anaesthesia Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare but catastrophic obstetric emergency with mortality rates of 20-60%. The classic presentation involves sudden cardiovascular collapse, acute respiratory failure, seizures, and DIC occurring during or shortly after labour and delivery. The pathophysiology involves an abnormal maternal immune response to foetal antigens entering the maternal circulation, triggering complement activation and consumptive coagulopathy. Management is entirely supportive: aggressive cardiopulmonary resuscitation, massive transfusion with early fibrinogen replacement, and consideration of perimortem caesarean if undelivered. There is no specific treatment.
key_points: AFE classically presents with the triad of cardiovascular collapse, respiratory failure, and DIC
Fibrinogen is the earliest coagulation factor to fall in obstetric DIC and should be replaced early
Target fibrinogen above 2.0 g/L in obstetric haemorrhage using cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate
AFE is a diagnosis of exclusion supported by the classic clinical syndrome
explanation.pearls: In obstetric DIC, fibrinogen below 2.0 g/L has a positive predictive value above 90% for severe postpartum haemorrhage - it falls earlier and more precipitously than other coagulation parameters and should be the primary monitoring target.

sources.label: NBA - Patient Blood Management Guideline: Obstetrics

stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a 5-year history of worsening dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia, and cyclical dyschezia with rectal bleeding during menstruation. She has been trying to conceive for 18 months without success. Previous transvaginal ultrasound showed a 4 cm left ovarian endometrioma. She has tried combined oral contraceptive pill and oral progestogens, which provided partial symptom relief but she ceased them to attempt pregnancy. On examination, there is a fixed retroverted uterus with tender nodularity in the uterosacral ligaments and rectovaginal septum.

data_table.Investigations: AMH 18 pmol/L (age-appropriate), CA-125 82 U/mL (ref < 35), day 3 FSH 7.2 IU/L, partner semen analysis normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management approach?

options.text: Resume combined oral contraceptive pill continuously for 6 months to suppress disease before natural conception attempts
options.rationale: While hormonal suppression improves symptoms, there is no evidence that a period of hormonal suppression prior to attempting conception improves subsequent fertility. This patient has been trying to conceive for 18 months and hormonal treatment further delays fertility investigation and treatment. (Source: RANZCOG - Endometriosis)

options.text: Referral to a multidisciplinary endometriosis centre for laparoscopic excision with concurrent fertility planning
options.rationale: This patient has deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) with evidence of rectovaginal and uterosacral involvement, an ovarian endometrioma, cyclical bowel symptoms suggesting bowel wall involvement, and 18 months of unexplained infertility. Management requires referral to a multidisciplinary endometriosis centre with advanced laparoscopic skills and colorectal surgical support. Surgical excision of DIE and cystectomy of the endometrioma can improve pain symptoms and natural conception rates. Concurrent fertility planning with reproductive medicine input is appropriate given her subfertility. (Source: RANZCOG - Endometriosis)

options.text: Commence GnRH agonist (leuprorelin) with add-back HRT for 6 months followed by IVF with no surgical intervention
options.rationale: GnRH agonist therapy suppresses endometriosis but does not improve fertility and further delays conception. A 4 cm endometrioma may also impair oocyte retrieval and ovarian response during IVF. Surgical treatment of the endometrioma before IVF is generally recommended when endometriomas are 4 cm or above. (Source: ESHRE - Endometriosis Guideline)

options.text: Proceed directly to IVF without surgical treatment to minimise further delay in achieving fertility this year
options.rationale: While IVF may ultimately be needed, proceeding directly without addressing the 4 cm endometrioma and deep infiltrating disease is suboptimal. Endometriomas 4 cm or larger can impair oocyte retrieval, and surgical excision of moderate-severe endometriosis has been shown to improve natural conception rates. A planned surgical approach with concurrent fertility planning optimises outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG - Endometriosis)

options.text: Trial of dienogest 2 mg daily for 3 months as targeted endometriosis treatment while continuing to attempt conception
options.rationale: Dienogest is an effective progestogen for endometriosis symptom control but is an oral progestogen that inhibits ovulation and is used as a contraceptive. Concurrent use while trying to conceive is contradictory. Hormonal treatments for endometriosis are not compatible with active fertility attempts. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Gynaecology)

explanation.summary: Deep infiltrating endometriosis is defined as endometriotic lesions penetrating more than 5 mm below the peritoneal surface, commonly involving the uterosacral ligaments, rectovaginal septum, and bowel wall. The combination of dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia, cyclical dyschezia with rectal bleeding, and a fixed retroverted uterus with rectovaginal nodularity is highly suggestive of DIE with bowel involvement. Management should occur at a multidisciplinary endometriosis centre with advanced laparoscopic and colorectal surgical expertise. Surgical excision improves both pain and fertility outcomes. AMH should be measured before endometrioma surgery as cystectomy can reduce ovarian reserve.
key_points: Cyclical dyschezia with rectal bleeding strongly suggests bowel endometriosis
DIE requires surgical management at a multidisciplinary centre with colorectal surgical support
Surgical excision of moderate-severe endometriosis improves natural conception rates
AMH measurement before endometrioma cystectomy helps counsel about ovarian reserve risk
