explanation.pearls: Endometrioma cystectomy (stripping technique) removes 1-6 mL of normal ovarian cortex with each procedure, potentially reducing AMH by 30-40% - bilateral cystectomy should only be performed by experienced surgeons with ovarian reserve implications discussed preoperatively.

stem: A 27-year-old woman at 14 weeks gestation following IVF presents with sudden onset right iliac fossa pain radiating to the right flank, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. She describes the pain as intermittent and colicky, having started 4 hours ago. She has a known 8 cm right ovarian cyst that was identified at her IVF egg collection and has been monitored. On examination, there is tenderness in the right iliac fossa with guarding.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 98 bpm, BP 115/72 mmHg, Temp 37.4 degrees C
data_table.Investigations: WCC 12.8 x10^9/L, CRP 18 mg/L, urinalysis negative, foetal heart rate on Doppler 158 bpm. Ultrasound: enlarged right ovary 9 cm with peripherally displaced follicles and absent intraovarian venous flow on Doppler, free fluid in pouch of Douglas

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Conservative management with analgesia and antiemetics, observation for 24 hours, and repeat ultrasound tomorrow
options.rationale: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency. Delayed surgery risks ovarian necrosis, peritonitis, and sepsis. The combination of sudden-onset colicky pain, enlarged ovary with absent venous flow on Doppler, and clinical features is highly suggestive of torsion. Conservative management is inappropriate when torsion is suspected. (Source: RANZCOG - Ovarian Masses in Pregnancy)

options.text: Arrange urgent appendicectomy as the most likely diagnosis is acute appendicitis given the right iliac fossa pain
options.rationale: While appendicitis is an important differential for right iliac fossa pain in pregnancy, the ultrasound findings of an enlarged ovary with absent venous flow and a known ovarian cyst make ovarian torsion the most likely diagnosis. The upward displacement of the appendix during pregnancy also makes the location atypical for appendicitis at 14 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG - Ovarian Masses in Pregnancy)

options.text: Urgent laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian conservation, performed by a surgeon experienced in laparoscopy during pregnancy
options.rationale: Ovarian torsion in pregnancy requires urgent surgical intervention. Laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian conservation is the standard approach regardless of the macroscopic appearance of the ovary, as ovarian recovery rates after detorsion exceed 90% even when the ovary appears dusky or black. Laparoscopy is safe in the first and second trimesters and is preferred over laparotomy. The cyst may be drained or excised if necessary, but the ovary should be preserved. (Source: RANZCOG - Ovarian Masses in Pregnancy)

options.text: Urgent laparotomy with right oophorectomy given the absent Doppler flow indicating likely complete ovarian necrosis
options.rationale: Oophorectomy should be avoided as the ovary has high recovery potential even when Doppler flow is absent and the ovary appears macroscopically compromised. Studies show ovarian viability rates above 90% after detorsion. Laparotomy is also unnecessarily invasive when laparoscopy is feasible. Ovarian preservation is especially important in this young woman who conceived through IVF. (Source: RANZCOG - Ovarian Masses in Pregnancy)

options.text: MRI pelvis to confirm the diagnosis before proceeding to surgery, with analgesia and antiemetics in the interim
options.rationale: When the clinical presentation and ultrasound findings are consistent with ovarian torsion, MRI should not delay surgical intervention. MRI has reasonable sensitivity for torsion but is not required when ultrasound shows absent venous flow in a symptomatic patient with a known risk factor. Time to surgery directly correlates with ovarian salvage rates. (Source: RANZCOG - Ovarian Masses in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion in pregnancy most commonly occurs in the first trimester and early second trimester, particularly in the context of ovarian hyperstimulation from IVF or large ovarian cysts. The classic presentation is sudden-onset colicky pelvic pain with nausea and vomiting. Ultrasound findings of an enlarged ovary with absent or reduced venous flow on Doppler support the diagnosis. Urgent laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian conservation is the treatment of choice - oophorectomy is almost never necessary and the ovary has remarkable recovery potential. In early pregnancy, loss of the corpus luteum-bearing ovary before 10-12 weeks may precipitate miscarriage unless exogenous progesterone is supplemented.
key_points: IVF-stimulated ovaries and large cysts are major risk factors for torsion in pregnancy
Absent venous flow on Doppler in a symptomatic patient is highly suggestive of torsion
Laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian conservation is standard regardless of ovarian appearance
Ovarian recovery rates after detorsion exceed 90% even when the ovary appears necrotic
explanation.pearls: The whirlpool sign on transvaginal ultrasound - a twisted vascular pedicle seen as a round hypoechoic structure with concentric internal echoes - is the most specific ultrasound finding for ovarian torsion, with specificity exceeding 95%.

sources.label: RCOG - Management of Ovarian Cysts in Pregnancy

stem: A 30-year-old woman at 29 weeks gestation presents with a gush of clear fluid from the vagina 2 hours ago. Sterile speculum examination reveals pooling of clear fluid in the posterior fornix. AmniSure test is positive. She has no contractions, no vaginal bleeding, and no fever. Foetal heart rate on CTG is 145 bpm with normal variability and no decelerations. Group B Streptococcus status is unknown.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 80 bpm, BP 116/74 mmHg, Temp 36.8 degrees C
data_table.Investigations: WCC 10.2 x10^9/L, CRP 4 mg/L, urine MCS pending, low vaginal swab taken

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management plan?

options.text: Immediate delivery by caesarean section given the risk of ascending infection at 29 weeks gestational age with ruptured membranes
options.rationale: At 29 weeks, the foetus is viable with good survival rates at tertiary centres. Immediate delivery would subject the neonate to significant prematurity-related morbidity. Expectant management with appropriate antibiotics and corticosteroids is the standard approach to gain time for foetal maturation while monitoring for complications. (Source: RANZCOG - PPROM)

options.text: Administer oral erythromycin 250 mg 6-hourly alone and plan delivery at 34 weeks if no complications arise in the interim
options.rationale: Erythromycin is appropriate prophylactic antibiotic therapy in PPROM (as per the ORACLE trial), but this answer omits the critical step of antenatal corticosteroids for foetal lung maturation. Additionally, without GBS status, GBS-specific intrapartum prophylaxis considerations are needed. (Source: RANZCOG - PPROM)

options.text: Commence IV amoxicillin and erythromycin for 48 hours then switch to oral, and administer corticosteroids for foetal lung maturation
options.rationale: This partially reflects the ORACLE protocol but is inaccurate. Australian practice follows erythromycin 250 mg 6-hourly for 10 days (or until delivery) as the prophylactic antibiotic. The amoxicillin-clavulanate combination was associated with increased necrotising enterocolitis risk in the ORACLE trial and should be avoided in PPROM. (Source: RANZCOG - PPROM)

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM now and repeat in 24 hours, commence erythromycin 250 mg 6-hourly for 10 days, and manage expectantly
options.rationale: Management of PPROM at 29 weeks involves: (1) antenatal corticosteroids - betamethasone 11.4 mg IM two doses 24 hours apart for foetal lung maturation, (2) prophylactic antibiotics - erythromycin 250 mg orally 6-hourly for 10 days per the ORACLE trial protocol, to prolong latency and reduce neonatal infection, (3) expectant management with regular maternal observations, CTG monitoring, and ultrasound surveillance, and (4) planned delivery at 36-37 weeks if no complications, or earlier if chorioamnionitis, non-reassuring foetal status, or significant abruption develops. (Source: RANZCOG - PPROM)

options.text: Administer magnesium sulfate 4 g IV loading dose for foetal neuroprotection, betamethasone for lungs, and plan delivery within 48 hours
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate for foetal neuroprotection is indicated when preterm delivery before 30 weeks is anticipated within 24 hours. This patient has PPROM without active labour, so delivery is not imminent. Planning delivery within 48 hours at 29 weeks would be premature when expectant management could safely extend the pregnancy by several weeks. (Source: RANZCOG - Magnesium Sulfate for Neuroprotection)

explanation.summary: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes (PPROM) at 24-36 weeks is managed with expectant management to prolong the latency period and reduce prematurity-related morbidity. The key interventions are antenatal corticosteroids for foetal lung maturation (betamethasone 11.4 mg IM x 2 doses 24 hours apart), prophylactic erythromycin for 10 days to prolong latency and reduce neonatal infection, and careful maternal and foetal surveillance. Amoxicillin-clavulanate should be avoided due to the association with neonatal necrotising enterocolitis in the ORACLE trial. Delivery is planned at 36-37 weeks unless complications develop earlier.
key_points: Antenatal corticosteroids are given between 24 and 34+6 weeks to promote foetal lung maturation
Erythromycin 250 mg 6-hourly for 10 days is the prophylactic antibiotic of choice in PPROM
Amoxicillin-clavulanate is avoided in PPROM due to increased neonatal NEC risk from the ORACLE trial
Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection is given when delivery before 30 weeks is imminent
explanation.pearls: The ORACLE trial showed erythromycin in PPROM reduced neonatal morbidity and prolonged latency by a median of 5 days, but amoxicillin-clavulanate was associated with a 4.6-fold increase in neonatal necrotising enterocolitis - this combination should never be used in PPROM.

stem: A 32-year-old woman with a monochorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy is seen at 20 weeks gestation for a routine ultrasound. Twin 1 has a deepest vertical pocket of amniotic fluid of 1.8 cm with a collapsed-appearing bladder. Twin 2 has a deepest vertical pocket of 12 cm with a distended bladder. Both twins have concordant estimated foetal weights. Umbilical artery Dopplers are normal in both twins.

data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound - Twin 1: DVP 1.8 cm (oligohydramnios), bladder not visualised, EFW 320 g (50th centile). Twin 2: DVP 12 cm (polyhydramnios), distended bladder, EFW 330 g (50th centile). Placenta anterior, single shared mass

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat ultrasound in 2 weeks as the discordance may be transient and the Dopplers are currently reassuring
options.rationale: This twin pair meets diagnostic criteria for twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS): monochorionic pregnancy with oligohydramnios in the donor twin (DVP below 2 cm) and polyhydramnios in the recipient twin (DVP above 8 cm). TTTS is progressive and has high mortality without intervention. Waiting 2 weeks risks rapid deterioration. (Source: RANZCOG - Twin Pregnancy Management)

options.text: Amnioreduction of the polyhydramniotic sac to reduce the deepest vertical pocket below 8 cm and relieve symptoms
options.rationale: Amnioreduction was previously used as treatment for TTTS but only addresses the symptom (polyhydramnios), not the underlying pathology (unbalanced placental vascular anastomoses). The Eurofetus trial demonstrated superior outcomes with fetoscopic laser ablation compared with serial amnioreduction. Amnioreduction is reserved for cases where laser therapy is unavailable. (Source: RANZCOG - Twin Pregnancy Management)

options.text: Selective foetal reduction of the donor twin to protect the recipient from the complications of TTTS
options.rationale: Selective reduction is not appropriate when both twins are alive with normal growth and normal Dopplers. Fetoscopic laser ablation addresses the underlying vascular anastomoses and preserves both twins in the majority of cases (86% survival of at least one twin). Reduction is considered only when one twin has severe compromise. (Source: RANZCOG - Twin Pregnancy Management)

options.text: Immediate delivery by caesarean section at 20 weeks as the condition is not compatible with continued pregnancy
options.rationale: Delivery at 20 weeks results in the loss of both twins as the foetuses are previable. TTTS is treatable with fetoscopic laser ablation, which has survival rates of at least one twin in 86% and both twins in 60-70% of cases. The aim is to treat and prolong the pregnancy to a viable gestational age. (Source: RANZCOG - Twin Pregnancy Management)

options.text: Urgent referral to a foetal medicine centre for fetoscopic laser photocoagulation of placental vascular anastomoses
options.rationale: This presentation meets Quintero stage II TTTS criteria (oligohydramnios-polyhydramnios sequence with non-visualised donor bladder). Fetoscopic laser photocoagulation of the placental vascular anastomoses is the treatment of choice for TTTS diagnosed between 16 and 26 weeks, as demonstrated by the Eurofetus trial. The procedure uses a fetoscope inserted into the polyhydramniotic sac to identify and ablate the inter-twin vascular anastomoses on the placental surface. (Source: RANZCOG - Twin Pregnancy Management)

explanation.summary: Twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome occurs in 10-15% of monochorionic diamniotic twin pregnancies due to unbalanced blood flow through placental vascular anastomoses. The donor twin develops oligohydramnios and a small or absent bladder, while the recipient develops polyhydramnios and a distended bladder. The Quintero staging system classifies severity from stage I (fluid discordance only) to stage V (foetal demise). Fetoscopic laser photocoagulation of placental anastomoses is the treatment of choice between 16 and 26 weeks, with survival of at least one twin in approximately 86% of cases. All monochorionic twins require 2-weekly ultrasound surveillance from 16 weeks.
key_points: TTTS diagnostic criteria - DVP below 2 cm in donor and above 8 cm in recipient in monochorionic twins
Fetoscopic laser ablation is superior to serial amnioreduction (Eurofetus trial)
Quintero staging guides prognosis - stages I-II have better outcomes than stages III-IV
All monochorionic twins require 2-weekly ultrasound from 16 weeks for early TTTS detection
explanation.pearls: In the Quintero staging system for TTTS, a non-visualised donor bladder marks stage II, absent or reversed end-diastolic flow in the umbilical artery marks stage III, and hydrops in either twin marks stage IV - each progressive stage carries significantly worse prognosis without intervention.

subtopic_detail: Vulval intraepithelial neoplasia and vulval cancer
stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with a 4-month history of a persistent, firm, raised vulval lesion on the left labium majus that has slowly enlarged. She reports intermittent pruritus and occasional bleeding when the lesion is irritated by clothing. She has a history of lichen sclerosus treated with topical clobetasol for 10 years. On examination, there is a 2.5 cm exophytic lesion on the left labium majus with irregular borders and contact bleeding. There are background white atrophic changes consistent with lichen sclerosus.

data_table.Examination: 2.5 cm firm exophytic lesion on left labium majus, irregular borders, contact bleeding, no palpable inguinofemoral lymphadenopathy, background lichen sclerosus changes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Punch biopsy of the lesion under local anaesthesia with referral to gynaecological oncology for results and management
options.rationale: A new or changing vulval lesion in the context of long-standing lichen sclerosus requires urgent biopsy to exclude vulval squamous cell carcinoma. Lichen sclerosus carries a 4-5% lifetime risk of vulval SCC, mediated through differentiated vulval intraepithelial neoplasia (dVIN). A punch biopsy from the most suspicious area under local anaesthesia provides tissue diagnosis. Gynaecological oncology referral is essential for management planning. (Source: RANZCOG - Vulval Disease)

options.text: Increase clobetasol to daily application for 3 months as the lesion is likely a hypertrophic lichen sclerosus plaque
options.rationale: While lichen sclerosus can cause hyperkeratotic plaques, a new exophytic lesion with irregular borders and contact bleeding in long-standing lichen sclerosus must be biopsied to exclude malignancy before assuming a benign diagnosis. Treating empirically with potent topical steroids would delay diagnosis of a potentially invasive cancer. (Source: RANZCOG - Vulval Disease)

options.text: Wide local excision of the lesion with 1 cm margins as both diagnostic and therapeutic procedure at this visit
options.rationale: Proceeding directly to excision without a tissue diagnosis is inappropriate as the surgical margins and approach differ depending on histology (dVIN, microinvasive SCC, frankly invasive SCC). Punch biopsy first determines the diagnosis and depth of invasion, which guides definitive surgical planning including the need for sentinel lymph node biopsy or inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy. (Source: Cancer Council Australia - Vulval Cancer Guidelines)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia - Vulval Cancer Guidelines

options.text: Apply topical imiquimod 5% cream three times weekly for 16 weeks for presumed HPV-related vulval intraepithelial neoplasia
options.rationale: Imiquimod is used for usual-type VIN (uVIN), which is HPV-related and typically occurs in younger women. This elderly patient with lichen sclerosus is at risk of differentiated VIN (dVIN) or invasive SCC, which is not HPV-related and does not respond to imiquimod. A tissue diagnosis is mandatory before any treatment. (Source: Cancer Council Australia - Vulval Cancer Guidelines)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia - Vulval Cancer Guidelines

options.text: Arrange MRI of the pelvis and inguinal regions to stage the lesion before proceeding with any local intervention
options.rationale: Imaging may be needed for staging if the biopsy confirms invasive cancer, but it should not delay tissue diagnosis. The appropriate sequence is biopsy first, then imaging and staging investigations based on the histological result. For small vulval lesions, clinical examination of inguinal nodes is the initial assessment. (Source: Cancer Council Australia - Vulval Cancer Guidelines)
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia - Vulval Cancer Guidelines

explanation.summary: Vulval squamous cell carcinoma is the most common vulval malignancy, with two distinct pathways: HPV-related (via usual-type VIN in younger women) and HPV-independent (via differentiated VIN in older women, often associated with lichen sclerosus). Lichen sclerosus carries a 4-5% lifetime risk of SCC. Any new, persistent, or changing vulval lesion in a patient with lichen sclerosus requires biopsy. Management of confirmed vulval SCC involves wide local excision with appropriate margins and sentinel lymph node biopsy or inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy depending on depth of invasion. Women with lichen sclerosus require lifelong vulval surveillance.
key_points: Lichen sclerosus carries a 4-5% lifetime risk of vulval SCC via the dVIN pathway
Any new or changing vulval lesion in lichen sclerosus requires punch biopsy
dVIN is HPV-independent and does not respond to imiquimod, unlike usual-type VIN
Tissue diagnosis must precede definitive surgery to plan appropriate margins and lymph node assessment
explanation.pearls: Differentiated VIN (dVIN) associated with lichen sclerosus has a much higher malignant transformation rate (up to 30-50%) than HPV-related usual-type VIN (uVIN, approximately 5-10%), making vigilant long-term surveillance of lichen sclerosus essential.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia - Vulval Cancer Guidelines

stem: A 10-month-old boy presents with 6 days of fever up to 39.5 degrees C that has not responded to paracetamol and ibuprofen. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection and cracked, erythematous lips. He has no rash, no cervical lymphadenopathy, and no extremity changes. He is irritable and refusing feeds. His older sibling had a viral upper respiratory tract infection last week.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR 36, Temp 39.2 degrees C, SpO2 99%
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.4 x10^9/L (neutrophils 14.2), platelets 480 x10^9/L, CRP 142 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/hr, ALT 68 U/L, albumin 28 g/L, urine microscopy - sterile pyuria (WCC 42/hpf), echocardiogram - mild coronary artery dilation (right coronary artery Z-score +2.8)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Continue supportive care and antipyretics as this is likely a prolonged viral illness given the household contact history
options.rationale: While a viral illness is a reasonable initial consideration, 6 days of high fever with conjunctival injection, mucositis, markedly elevated inflammatory markers, sterile pyuria, and coronary artery dilation is not consistent with a simple viral illness. The echocardiographic abnormality confirms cardiac involvement consistent with Kawasaki disease. (Source: RCH CPG - Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Administer IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as a single infusion over 10-12 hours with high-dose aspirin 30-50 mg/kg/day in divided doses
options.rationale: This infant meets criteria for incomplete Kawasaki disease: fever for 5 or more days with only 2 of 5 classic clinical features (conjunctival injection and lip changes), but with supportive laboratory findings (elevated CRP and ESR, leucocytosis, thrombocytosis, hypoalbuminaemia, elevated ALT, sterile pyuria) and echocardiographic evidence of coronary involvement. IVIG 2 g/kg within 10 days of fever onset significantly reduces coronary aneurysm risk. High-dose aspirin is given until afebrile for 48-72 hours, then low-dose aspirin 3-5 mg/kg/day. (Source: RCH CPG - Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone for possible occult bacteraemia given the prolonged fever and elevated inflammatory markers
options.rationale: While occult bacteraemia is a consideration in a febrile infant, the combination of specific clinical features (conjunctival injection, mucositis), supportive laboratory findings, and coronary artery changes points to Kawasaki disease rather than bacterial infection. Blood cultures should be taken but empiric antibiotics do not address the vasculitic process. (Source: RCH CPG - Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Observe for 48 more hours to see if additional clinical features develop to meet full Kawasaki criteria before treating
options.rationale: Waiting for full diagnostic criteria in suspected incomplete Kawasaki disease is inappropriate as it delays treatment and increases coronary aneurysm risk. The AHA algorithm for incomplete Kawasaki disease recommends treatment when supportive laboratory and echocardiographic findings are present. This is particularly important in infants under 12 months. (Source: AHA - Kawasaki Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day as primary treatment with low-dose aspirin and serial echocardiography monitoring
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not first-line treatment for Kawasaki disease. IVIG is the standard first-line treatment. Corticosteroids may be considered as adjunctive therapy in patients at high risk of IVIG resistance (e.g., Kobayashi score), but they do not replace IVIG. (Source: RCH CPG - Kawasaki Disease)

explanation.summary: Incomplete or atypical Kawasaki disease presents with fewer than 4 of the 5 classic clinical features but is particularly common in infants under 12 months - the age group at highest risk for coronary complications. The AHA algorithm recommends treatment when a child has prolonged fever with 2-3 clinical features and supportive laboratory or echocardiographic findings. Key laboratory supports include CRP 30 mg/L or above, ESR 40 mm/hr or above, hypoalbuminaemia, anaemia, thrombocytosis after day 7, elevated ALT, and sterile pyuria. Any echocardiographic coronary abnormality in this context mandates treatment. IVIG within the first 10 days of illness reduces the incidence of coronary artery aneurysms from 25% to less than 5%.
key_points: Infants under 12 months are at highest risk for incomplete Kawasaki disease and coronary complications
Incomplete Kawasaki disease is diagnosed using supportive laboratory and echocardiographic criteria
IVIG 2 g/kg as a single infusion within 10 days of fever onset is the standard treatment
Sterile pyuria on urine microscopy is a supportive finding often overlooked in the workup
explanation.pearls: A coronary artery Z-score above +2.5 in any segment during a febrile illness in a child should prompt immediate consideration of Kawasaki disease regardless of how many classic clinical features are present - echocardiographic abnormalities mandate treatment.

stem: A 5-month-old infant is brought to the emergency department by his mother with a swollen left thigh noticed during a nappy change. The mother states the infant has been unsettled for the past 2 days and she assumed he was teething. There is no reported history of trauma. The infant was born at term with no neonatal complications. He is not yet pulling to stand or cruising. X-ray of the left femur shows a spiral fracture of the mid-shaft. You also notice fading bruises on both ears.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 152 bpm, RR 38, Temp 37.0 degrees C, weight 7.2 kg (50th centile)
data_table.Examination: Left thigh swollen and tender to palpation, fading yellow-green bruises on bilateral ear pinnae, no other bruises, anterior fontanelle soft and flat, fundoscopy normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Apply a splint to the left thigh and discharge with outpatient orthopaedic follow-up in 1 week for the fracture
options.rationale: Discharging this infant without further investigation is unsafe. A spiral femoral fracture in a pre-mobile 5-month-old with no history of trauma and bruising to the ears raises very high suspicion for non-accidental injury. The child must be admitted for safety and further evaluation. (Source: RCH CPG - Child Abuse)

options.text: Reassure the mother that spiral femoral fractures are common in infants learning to roll and manage with a hip spica
options.rationale: Spiral femoral fractures are not a normal consequence of rolling in a 5-month-old. Femoral fractures in children under 18 months who are not yet independently mobile carry a very high positive predictive value for non-accidental injury. The ear bruising adds further concern as ears are a protected site. (Source: RCH CPG - Child Abuse)

options.text: Admit the infant, perform skeletal survey and bloods including FBC, coagulation, calcium, phosphate, and ALP, and notify the child protection team
options.rationale: This presentation has multiple features highly concerning for non-accidental injury: femoral fracture in a pre-mobile infant with no history of trauma, and bruising to the ears (a protected site). Management includes: (1) admit for safety, (2) full skeletal survey to identify occult fractures, (3) blood tests including FBC, coagulation screen, calcium, phosphate, ALP, and vitamin D to exclude medical causes of fractures, (4) notify the hospital child protection unit, and (5) make a mandatory child protection report. (Source: RCH CPG - Child Abuse)

options.text: Order genetic testing for osteogenesis imperfecta and defer child protection involvement until results are available
options.rationale: While osteogenesis imperfecta should be considered in the differential, genetic testing takes weeks and must not delay child protection assessment. The combination of fracture pattern, pre-mobile age, no history of trauma, and ear bruising mandates immediate child protection involvement. Investigations for medical causes occur concurrently. (Source: RCH CPG - Child Abuse)

options.text: Contact police directly without involving the hospital child protection team, and arrange foster care before investigation
options.rationale: While police involvement may be appropriate, the correct pathway is through the hospital child protection team who coordinate the multidisciplinary response including medical assessment, social work evaluation, police liaison, and mandatory reporting. The medical team's role is to ensure the child's safety, complete investigations, and report. (Source: RCH CPG - Child Abuse)

explanation.summary: Non-accidental injury must be considered when the injury pattern is inconsistent with the reported mechanism and the child's developmental stage. Key red flags include fractures in pre-mobile infants, bruising in non-ambulatory children, bruises on protected sites (ears, neck, trunk in non-mobile infants), multiple fractures of different ages, and inconsistent or changing histories. The medical workup includes skeletal survey, ophthalmological examination, and blood tests to exclude medical mimics. All Australian states and territories have mandatory reporting requirements for health professionals.
key_points: Femoral fractures in pre-mobile infants have a very high positive predictive value for NAI
Bruising on ears, neck, and trunk in non-ambulatory infants is highly suspicious for abuse
Skeletal survey is mandatory to identify occult fractures in suspected NAI
Investigations for medical causes must run concurrently with child protection involvement
explanation.pearls: The TEN-4-FACESp clinical decision rule for bruising identifies bruising to the Torso, Ears, Neck in children under 4 years, or any bruising in infants under 4 months, plus Frenulum, Angle of jaw, Cheeks, Eyelids, Subconjunctivae, and patterned bruises as high-risk indicators for abuse.

sources.label: RACP - Health Professionals and Child Protection

stem: A 3-day-old term male infant, birth weight 4.6 kg, born to a non-diabetic mother, has had persistent hypoglycaemia despite maximum enteral feeding and IV dextrose at 12 mg/kg/min. He was macrosomic at birth with no dysmorphic features. He has had 4 episodes of blood glucose below 2.0 mmol/L in the past 24 hours despite escalating IV dextrose. A critical sample was collected during a hypoglycaemic episode.

data_table.Investigations: Critical sample results - blood glucose 1.4 mmol/L, serum insulin 42 mIU/L (ref < 2 when glucose < 2.6), C-peptide 1.8 nmol/L (elevated), cortisol 420 nmol/L, growth hormone 18 mIU/L, ketones 0.1 mmol/L, free fatty acids 0.08 mmol/L (low), ammonia 32 micromol/L (ref < 50), lactate 1.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Increase IV dextrose concentration and rate to achieve glucose delivery of 15 mg/kg/min with continuous glucagon infusion
options.rationale: While increasing glucose delivery and glucagon infusion may temporarily manage hypoglycaemia, this does not address the underlying cause. The critical sample confirms hyperinsulinism (inappropriately elevated insulin during hypoglycaemia, suppressed ketones and free fatty acids). Specific medical treatment with diazoxide should be commenced. (Source: RCH CPG - Neonatal Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Commence oral hydrocortisone 5 mg/m2/day in three divided doses for suspected cortisol deficiency causing hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: The cortisol level of 420 nmol/L during hypoglycaemia is an appropriate stress response, excluding cortisol deficiency as the cause. The critical sample clearly demonstrates hyperinsulinism - high insulin with suppressed counter-regulatory responses. Hydrocortisone is not indicated. (Source: RCH CPG - Neonatal Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Commence dietary management with cornstarch supplementation and frequent high-protein feeds to stabilise glucose
options.rationale: Cornstarch supplementation is used in glycogen storage disorders and older children with hypoglycaemia, not as primary treatment for congenital hyperinsulinism in neonates. The severity of this infant's hypoglycaemia (requiring 12 mg/kg/min glucose) necessitates pharmacological treatment. (Source: RCH CPG - Neonatal Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Commence diazoxide 5-15 mg/kg/day in divided doses with chlorothiazide, and refer to a paediatric endocrinologist for further evaluation
options.rationale: The critical sample confirms congenital hyperinsulinism: inappropriately elevated insulin (42 mIU/L) during hypoglycaemia (glucose 1.4 mmol/L), with suppressed ketones and free fatty acids indicating insulin-mediated suppression of lipolysis and ketogenesis. Diazoxide is the first-line medical treatment - it opens KATP channels on beta cells to inhibit insulin secretion. Chlorothiazide is added to counteract fluid retention. Response to diazoxide guides further management including genetic testing. (Source: RCH CPG - Neonatal Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Arrange urgent pancreatic MRI and plan for near-total pancreatectomy given the severity of hypoglycaemia requiring high glucose
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for congenital hyperinsulinism that is unresponsive to medical therapy (diazoxide-unresponsive disease). Medical management must be trialled first. Additionally, standard MRI cannot distinguish focal from diffuse disease - 18F-DOPA PET-CT is the specialised imaging modality used to localise focal lesions. (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Congenital Hyperinsulinism)

explanation.summary: Congenital hyperinsulinism is the most common cause of persistent hypoglycaemia in neonates and infants. The critical sample during hypoglycaemia is diagnostic: inappropriately detectable insulin (above 2 mIU/L when glucose below 2.6 mmol/L), suppressed ketones and free fatty acids, and an elevated glucose requirement (above 8 mg/kg/min). Diazoxide is first-line treatment, with response predicting diffuse KATP channel disease (responsive) versus focal disease (often unresponsive). Diazoxide-unresponsive disease requires genetic testing and 18F-DOPA PET-CT to distinguish focal from diffuse disease, as focal lesions can be cured by limited pancreatectomy.
key_points: Glucose requirement above 8 mg/kg/min suggests hyperinsulinism as the cause of neonatal hypoglycaemia
The hypoketotic hypoglycaemia pattern is the hallmark of insulin-mediated hypoglycaemia
Diazoxide is first-line medical treatment acting on KATP channels to suppress insulin secretion
18F-DOPA PET-CT localises focal disease amenable to curative limited pancreatectomy
explanation.pearls: During a hypoglycaemic episode, a glucose rise of more than 1.5 mmol/L within 30 minutes of IM glucagon 0.5 mg confirms glycogen-replete hyperinsulinism - this glucagon stimulation test can be diagnostic even before the critical sample results return.

stem: A 12-day-old infant born at term presents to the emergency department with poor feeding, lethargy, and weight loss since birth (birth weight 3.4 kg, current weight 2.9 kg). The infant was born with ambiguous genitalia - apparent clitoromegaly with partially fused labioscrotal folds and a single perineal opening - but the family was told to wait for the newborn screening result. The newborn screening result is not yet available. The infant appears dehydrated and peripherally shut down with poor capillary refill.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 192 bpm, RR 52, Temp 36.2 degrees C, SpO2 97%, CRT 5 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 118 mmol/L, potassium 8.2 mmol/L, glucose 1.8 mmol/L, urea 12.4 mmol/L, creatinine 62 micromol/L, venous pH 7.22, bicarbonate 14 mmol/L, lactate 5.8 mmol/L, ECG shows peaked T waves and widened QRS complexes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg bolus, IV 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg for hypoglycaemia, and commence IV hydrocortisone 25 mg stat with urgent endocrine consult
options.rationale: While fluid resuscitation, glucose correction, and hydrocortisone are all appropriate, this answer omits the critical emergency treatment of life-threatening hyperkalaemia. With potassium of 8.2 mmol/L and ECG changes (peaked T waves and widened QRS), hyperkalaemia must be addressed immediately to prevent cardiac arrest. Calcium gluconate for cardiac membrane stabilisation is the immediate priority. (Source: RCH CPG - Adrenal Crisis)

options.text: Commence IV insulin-dextrose infusion to correct the hyperkalaemia as the primary emergency treatment before any further investigation or workup
options.rationale: While insulin-dextrose is a treatment for hyperkalaemia, it is not the first-line immediate intervention when ECG changes are present. IV calcium gluconate stabilises the cardiac membrane within minutes, providing immediate cardioprotection. Additionally, this approach fails to address the underlying adrenal crisis with hydrocortisone. (Source: RCH CPG - Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Oral sodium chloride supplementation and fludrocortisone, recheck electrolytes in 4 hours, and await newborn screening results before starting steroids
options.rationale: This infant is in acute adrenal crisis with shock, life-threatening hyperkalaemia with ECG changes, severe hyponatraemia, and hypoglycaemia. Oral supplementation is grossly inadequate. Waiting for newborn screening results before treating could be fatal. (Source: RCH CPG - Adrenal Crisis)

options.text: Arrange urgent karyotype and pelvic ultrasound to determine chromosomal sex before initiating any treatment for the presenting adrenal crisis
options.rationale: While sex determination is important in the longer-term management of ambiguous genitalia and suspected CAH, it has no bearing on the acute emergency management of adrenal crisis. This infant requires immediate resuscitation for shock, hyperkalaemia, hyponatraemia, and hypoglycaemia. (Source: RCH CPG - Adrenal Crisis)

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10% for cardiac stabilisation, IV 0.9% saline 20 mL/kg bolus, IV dextrose, IV hydrocortisone 25 mg stat, and send 17-OHP before steroid
options.rationale: This infant presents with a salt-wasting adrenal crisis from presumed congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency), evidenced by ambiguous genitalia, severe hyponatraemia, life-threatening hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, and shock. Management priorities in order: (1) IV calcium gluconate for immediate cardiac membrane stabilisation given ECG changes, (2) IV normal saline bolus for volume resuscitation, (3) IV dextrose for hypoglycaemia, (4) IV hydrocortisone 25 mg stat. Ideally, a 17-hydroxyprogesterone level is drawn before hydrocortisone but must not delay treatment. (Source: RCH CPG - Adrenal Crisis)

explanation.summary: Salt-wasting congenital adrenal hyperplasia (most commonly 21-hydroxylase deficiency) presents in the first 1-3 weeks of life with adrenal crisis in severe cases. The triad of ambiguous genitalia in a 46,XX infant, hyponatraemia with hyperkalaemia, and cortisol deficiency is diagnostic. The salt-wasting crisis occurs because both cortisol and aldosterone production are impaired. Life-threatening hyperkalaemia with ECG changes requires immediate calcium gluconate for cardiac membrane stabilisation. IV hydrocortisone at stress doses provides both glucocorticoid replacement and, at high doses, sufficient mineralocorticoid activity until fludrocortisone can be started orally.
key_points: Salt-wasting CAH typically presents at days 7-14 of life when maternal steroid influence wanes
Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes requires immediate IV calcium gluconate before other interventions
IV hydrocortisone at stress doses provides both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid effect
17-hydroxyprogesterone should be drawn before hydrocortisone if possible but must not delay treatment
explanation.pearls: In 21-hydroxylase deficiency, 17-hydroxyprogesterone levels are typically massively elevated (above 300 nmol/L, often above 1000 nmol/L) - the newborn screening test detects this, but results may not be available before the salt-wasting crisis presents at 7-14 days of life.

sources.label: Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group - CAH Guidelines
BMJ Best Practice - Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia

stem: A 62-year-old man presents to the emergency department with rigors, confusion, and productive cough for 2 days. He has a history of type 2 diabetes and COPD. He was found by his wife lying on the couch, disoriented and unable to stand. He has not been taking fluids for the past 24 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118 bpm, BP 82/48 mmHg, RR 28, Temp 38.9 degrees C, SpO2 91% on room air, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6)
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22.4 x10^9/L (neutrophils 19.8), CRP 284 mg/L, lactate 4.8 mmol/L, creatinine 168 micromol/L (baseline 92), glucose 18.2 mmol/L, chest X-ray - right lower lobe consolidation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Obtain blood cultures from 2 peripheral sites, commence IV ceftriaxone 1 g plus azithromycin 500 mg, administer 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid, and remeasure lactate
options.rationale: This patient has sepsis (suspected infection with organ dysfunction) likely secondary to community-acquired pneumonia. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle mandates: (1) blood cultures before antibiotics, (2) broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, (3) IV crystalloid 30 mL/kg for hypotension or lactate 4 mmol/L or above, (4) reassess haemodynamic status and lactate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence IV flucloxacillin 2 g and gentamicin 5 mg/kg, obtain blood cultures, and arrange CT chest with contrast urgently
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin plus gentamicin is an inappropriate antibiotic combination for community-acquired pneumonia. Flucloxacillin covers Staphylococcus aureus but not the typical CAP pathogens. Ceftriaxone plus azithromycin is the Australian guideline-recommended regimen for severe CAP. CT chest is not urgently needed when CXR shows consolidation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic)

options.text: Administer IV normal saline 500 mL over 2 hours, commence oral amoxicillin 1 g, and observe in the emergency short stay unit
options.rationale: This patient has sepsis with hypotension and elevated lactate, requiring aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg) and IV antibiotics. Oral amoxicillin alone is appropriate for mild CAP, not severe sepsis. A 500 mL infusion over 2 hours is grossly inadequate. (Source: ANZICS - Sepsis Management)

options.text: Immediately commence noradrenaline infusion via peripheral cannula while arranging central venous access and ICU admission
options.rationale: Vasopressors should not be commenced before adequate volume resuscitation. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign recommends 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid as the initial resuscitation fluid, with vasopressors added if hypotension persists despite fluid resuscitation. (Source: ANZICS - Sepsis Management)

options.text: Obtain sputum culture, commence IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g, and arrange urgent bronchoscopy for directed therapy
options.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam is broader than necessary for initial empiric treatment of severe CAP in a patient without healthcare-associated risk factors. Ceftriaxone plus azithromycin provides appropriate coverage. Urgent bronchoscopy is not indicated and would delay essential resuscitation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Sepsis is defined as life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle includes: measuring lactate, obtaining blood cultures before antibiotics, administering broad-spectrum antibiotics, beginning rapid crystalloid infusion for hypotension or elevated lactate, and applying vasopressors if hypotension persists. For severe community-acquired pneumonia in Australia, the recommended empiric regimen is IV ceftriaxone plus azithromycin. Early recognition and aggressive management within the first hour significantly reduce mortality.
key_points: Blood cultures must be obtained before antibiotics but should not delay antibiotic administration beyond 1 hour
30 mL/kg IV crystalloid is the recommended initial fluid bolus for sepsis-induced hypotension
Lactate above 4 mmol/L indicates tissue hypoperfusion and triggers aggressive fluid resuscitation
Ceftriaxone plus azithromycin is the Australian guideline-recommended empiric regimen for severe CAP
explanation.pearls: Serial lactate measurements (at least every 2-4 hours) guide resuscitation adequacy in sepsis - a lactate clearance of at least 20% in the first 2 hours is associated with improved mortality outcomes.

stem: A 42-year-old woman is referred by her GP for evaluation of suspected Cushing syndrome. She has had progressive weight gain of 15 kg over 18 months, predominantly truncal, with new-onset proximal muscle weakness, easy bruising, and wide purple striae on her abdomen. She developed type 2 diabetes 6 months ago requiring insulin and hypertension requiring 3 antihypertensives. She takes no exogenous glucocorticoids.

data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 68 mmol/mol, fasting glucose 11.2 mmol/L, potassium 3.0 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial biochemical investigation to confirm the diagnosis?

options.text: Random morning serum cortisol level to screen for elevated basal cortisol production in this patient
options.rationale: A random morning cortisol has very poor sensitivity and specificity for Cushing syndrome because cortisol has a pulsatile diurnal secretion pattern and can be normal even in Cushing syndrome. It does not assess the loss of normal diurnal variation or suppressibility. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Endocrinology)

options.text: Overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test with measurement of 8 am serum cortisol the following morning
options.rationale: The overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test is one of the recommended first-line screening tests for Cushing syndrome. The patient takes dexamethasone 1 mg at 11 pm and serum cortisol is measured at 8 am the next morning. A cortisol level below 50 nmol/L essentially excludes Cushing syndrome. Abnormal results require confirmation with additional tests. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Endocrinology)

options.text: MRI of the pituitary gland with gadolinium to identify a pituitary adenoma as the source of excess cortisol
options.rationale: Pituitary MRI is used to localise the source of ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome AFTER biochemical confirmation. Imaging before biochemical confirmation risks identifying incidental pituitary lesions (present in up to 10% of the population) and leading to inappropriate surgery. (Source: Endocrine Society - Cushing Syndrome Guidelines)

options.text: Plasma ACTH level to distinguish between ACTH-dependent and ACTH-independent causes of hypercortisolism
options.rationale: Plasma ACTH measurement is the second step in the diagnostic workup, used to differentiate ACTH-dependent from ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome. It is performed after biochemical confirmation of hypercortisolism, not as the initial screening test. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Endocrinology)

options.text: High-dose 8 mg dexamethasone suppression test to differentiate pituitary from ectopic ACTH secretion directly
options.rationale: The high-dose dexamethasone suppression test is a later step used to distinguish pituitary Cushing disease from ectopic ACTH secretion. It is performed after hypercortisolism is confirmed and ACTH-dependence established, not as an initial screening test. (Source: Endocrine Society - Cushing Syndrome Guidelines)

explanation.summary: The diagnostic workup for Cushing syndrome follows a stepwise approach: (1) confirm hypercortisolism with at least two concordant first-line screening tests (overnight 1 mg DST, 24-hour urinary free cortisol, or late-night salivary cortisol), (2) determine ACTH-dependence with plasma ACTH measurement, (3) localise the source with imaging, and (4) differentiate pituitary from ectopic ACTH if needed. Exogenous glucocorticoid use must be excluded before testing. The clinical features in this case are highly suggestive of endogenous Cushing syndrome.
key_points: The 1 mg overnight DST is the most widely used first-line screening test for Cushing syndrome
At least two concordant positive screening tests are needed before proceeding to further investigation
Exogenous glucocorticoid use including inhaled, topical, and intra-articular must be excluded before testing
Imaging should only be performed after biochemical confirmation to avoid incidentaloma confusion
explanation.pearls: The 1 mg overnight DST has a false-positive rate of approximately 5% - common causes include depression, alcohol excess, obesity, oral oestrogen therapy (increases cortisol-binding globulin), and shift work disrupting the HPA axis circadian rhythm.

stem: A 48-year-old man was diagnosed with an unprovoked left leg proximal deep vein thrombosis 3 months ago and commenced on rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily for 3 weeks followed by 20 mg daily. He has had no bleeding complications. He has no identifiable risk factors - no recent immobilisation, surgery, travel, malignancy, or thrombophilia on testing. His BMI is 24 kg/m2 and he is otherwise well.

data_table.Investigations: Compression ultrasound - residual non-compressible thrombus in the left popliteal vein but improving. FBC, UEC, LFTs normal. Cancer screening up to date and negative. Thrombophilia screen - negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate recommendation regarding anticoagulation duration?

options.text: Cease anticoagulation now as 3 months is the standard recommended duration for all proximal DVT events
options.rationale: Three months is the minimum duration for proximal DVT, but the decision to extend depends on whether the VTE was provoked or unprovoked, bleeding risk, and patient preference. Unprovoked proximal DVT carries a 10% annual recurrence risk after cessation. Extended therapy is generally recommended if bleeding risk is acceptable. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Haematology)

options.text: Continue warfarin with target INR 2-3 indefinitely, as warfarin is the preferred agent for long-term prevention
options.rationale: DOACs are preferred over warfarin for most patients with VTE due to non-inferior efficacy, lower bleeding risk, and no requirement for INR monitoring. There is no reason to switch this patient from rivaroxaban to warfarin. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Haematology)

options.text: Continue rivaroxaban at a reduced dose of 10 mg daily for extended secondary prevention, with annual review of bleeding risk
options.rationale: For unprovoked proximal DVT with low bleeding risk, extended anticoagulation is recommended. After the initial treatment period, DOAC dose reduction for extended secondary prevention is supported by the EINSTEIN CHOICE trial (rivaroxaban 10 mg daily). These reduced doses maintain significant recurrence reduction with a lower bleeding profile. Annual reassessment is essential. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Haematology)

options.text: Cease anticoagulation and commence aspirin 100 mg daily for long-term secondary prevention of recurrent VTE
options.rationale: Aspirin provides modest (approximately 30%) reduction in VTE recurrence, significantly inferior to reduced-dose DOAC therapy (approximately 70-80% reduction). Aspirin is considered only when anticoagulation is contraindicated or refused. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Haematology)

options.text: Cease anticoagulation at 6 months and perform D-dimer testing 4 weeks later to guide need for extended therapy
options.rationale: While elevated D-dimer after cessation is associated with increased recurrence risk, current Australian practice for unprovoked proximal DVT with low bleeding risk favours extended anticoagulation rather than a stop-and-test strategy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Haematology)

explanation.summary: The duration of anticoagulation for VTE depends primarily on whether the event was provoked or unprovoked and the patient's bleeding risk. Provoked VTE requires 3 months. Unprovoked proximal DVT carries a high recurrence risk after cessation, and extended therapy is recommended if bleeding risk is acceptable. After the initial treatment period, dose reduction to rivaroxaban 10 mg daily or apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily provides effective secondary prevention as demonstrated in the EINSTEIN CHOICE and AMPLIFY-EXT trials.
key_points: Unprovoked proximal DVT carries approximately 10% annual recurrence risk after stopping anticoagulation
Extended DOAC therapy at reduced dose is recommended for unprovoked VTE with low bleeding risk
Rivaroxaban 10 mg daily or apixaban 2.5 mg BD are the evidence-based reduced doses for extended prevention
Annual reassessment of bleeding risk and patient preference should guide continuation decisions
explanation.pearls: The EINSTEIN CHOICE trial demonstrated that rivaroxaban 10 mg daily reduced VTE recurrence by 70% compared with aspirin, with no significant increase in major bleeding - making reduced-dose DOAC the preferred extended prevention strategy.

sources.label: CHEST Guidelines - Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE

stem: A 58-year-old man presents with 18 hours of worsening headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, and confusion. His wife reports he was well yesterday morning. He has a history of splenectomy following a motor vehicle accident 15 years ago and receives annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccinations, though he is unsure if his pneumococcal vaccine is current.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 112 bpm, BP 138/82 mmHg, RR 22, Temp 39.4 degrees C, GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5)
data_table.Investigations: WCC 24.6 x10^9/L, CRP 312 mg/L, platelets 168 x10^9/L. CT brain normal. CSF - opening pressure 32 cmH2O, WCC 2400/microL (95% neutrophils), protein 4.2 g/L, glucose 0.8 mmol/L (serum glucose 7.2), Gram stain - Gram-positive diplococci

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antimicrobial and adjunctive therapy?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly alone, with repeat lumbar puncture in 48 hours to confirm sterilisation of the CSF
options.rationale: While ceftriaxone is appropriate, this answer omits adjunctive dexamethasone (which reduces mortality in pneumococcal meningitis) and vancomycin (needed empirically in a post-splenectomy patient to cover penicillin-resistant pneumococci). (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic)

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 2.4 g 4-hourly with adjunctive IV dexamethasone 10 mg 6-hourly for 4 days starting immediately
options.rationale: While benzylpenicillin covers penicillin-sensitive pneumococci and dexamethasone is appropriate, penicillin alone does not provide adequate coverage for potentially resistant organisms. In a post-splenectomy patient, the consequences of treatment failure are catastrophic. Empiric therapy should include ceftriaxone plus vancomycin. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic)

options.text: IV meropenem 2 g 8-hourly with IV dexamethasone 10 mg 6-hourly for 4 days commenced before the first antibiotic dose
options.rationale: Meropenem is not first-line empiric therapy for community-acquired bacterial meningitis. It is reserved for specific situations such as Listeria cover or beta-lactam allergy scenarios. The standard empiric regimen is ceftriaxone plus vancomycin. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic)

options.text: IV dexamethasone 10 mg immediately, then IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly plus IV vancomycin, dexamethasone continued for 4 days
options.rationale: The CSF confirms pneumococcal meningitis. Management requires: (1) IV dexamethasone 10 mg given before or with the first dose of antibiotics, which reduces mortality by 50% in pneumococcal meningitis (de Gans trial), (2) IV ceftriaxone as the primary antibiotic, (3) IV vancomycin added empirically for potential penicillin resistance in this asplenic patient, and (4) dexamethasone continued for 4 days. Dexamethasone should be ceased if a non-pneumococcal organism is identified. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly plus IV ampicillin 2 g 4-hourly for Listeria coverage, with dexamethasone 10 mg 6-hourly for 4 days
options.rationale: Ampicillin for Listeria is indicated in patients over 50 or immunocompromised, but the Gram stain shows Gram-positive diplococci - diagnostic for Streptococcus pneumoniae, not Listeria (Gram-positive rods). Vancomycin to cover potential resistance is more important than Listeria coverage given the confirmed organism. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Adult bacterial meningitis is a medical emergency requiring immediate empiric antibiotics and adjunctive dexamethasone. Dexamethasone commenced before or with the first antibiotic dose reduces mortality by approximately 50% in pneumococcal meningitis. Empiric therapy with ceftriaxone plus vancomycin covers both sensitive and resistant pneumococci. Asplenia is a major risk factor for overwhelming post-splenectomy infection from encapsulated organisms, with Streptococcus pneumoniae being the most common pathogen.
key_points: Dexamethasone must be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics for pneumococcal meningitis
Empiric ceftriaxone plus vancomycin provides coverage for penicillin-resistant pneumococci
CSF glucose to serum glucose ratio below 0.4 strongly suggests bacterial meningitis
Asplenic patients are at extreme risk of OPSI from encapsulated organisms
explanation.pearls: The survival benefit of adjunctive dexamethasone in bacterial meningitis is only proven for Streptococcus pneumoniae - if cultures identify a different organism, dexamethasone should be discontinued as it may reduce CNS antibiotic penetration without providing benefit.

sources.label: IDSA - Practice Guidelines for Bacterial Meningitis
de Gans J et al. NEJM 2002 - Dexamethasone in Bacterial Meningitis

stem: A 55-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after being found confused and hypotensive at home. She has a known history of Addison disease diagnosed 8 years ago and takes hydrocortisone 10 mg morning / 5 mg afternoon and fludrocortisone 100 mcg daily. Her partner reports she developed gastroenteritis 3 days ago with vomiting and diarrhoea and was unable to keep her tablets down. She did not have an emergency injection kit at home.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124 bpm, BP 72/44 mmHg, RR 24, Temp 38.2 degrees C, GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5)
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 122 mmol/L, potassium 6.4 mmol/L, glucose 2.8 mmol/L, urea 14.8 mmol/L, creatinine 148 micromol/L, cortisol 28 nmol/L, TSH 4.8 mIU/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Resume oral hydrocortisone at double the usual dose with oral fludrocortisone and IV fluid support for rehydration of the patient
options.rationale: This patient is in acute adrenal crisis with shock, reduced consciousness, and inability to tolerate oral medications. Oral replacement is completely inadequate. Parenteral stress-dose hydrocortisone is required urgently. Doubling the usual oral dose is for minor illness only. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Endocrinology)

options.text: IV dexamethasone 4 mg as the preferred glucocorticoid in adrenal crisis because of its longer duration of action than hydrocortisone
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is the preferred glucocorticoid in adrenal crisis because it provides both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid activity at stress doses. Dexamethasone has no mineralocorticoid activity and would not address the aldosterone deficiency component of primary adrenal insufficiency. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Endocrinology)

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 50 mg stat, IV normal saline with 5% dextrose, and commence vasopressors immediately before fluid loading is complete
options.rationale: The hydrocortisone dose is suboptimal (100 mg is the standard stress dose, not 50 mg) and commencing vasopressors before adequate volume resuscitation is incorrect. Hypotension in adrenal crisis is primarily due to volume depletion. Volume resuscitation is the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Endocrinology)

options.text: Administer IV fludrocortisone equivalent for the mineralocorticoid deficiency as the primary treatment with IV fluid support for volume
options.rationale: There is no IV formulation of fludrocortisone. IV hydrocortisone at stress doses (100 mg) provides sufficient mineralocorticoid activity to address the aldosterone deficiency in the acute setting. Oral fludrocortisone is resumed once the patient can tolerate oral medications. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Endocrinology)

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat then 50 mg 6-hourly, rapid IV 0.9% sodium chloride bolus, IV dextrose, and treat the precipitating cause
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis management requires: (1) IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat followed by 50 mg IV 6-8 hourly, (2) aggressive IV 0.9% sodium chloride (typically 1 L in the first hour), (3) IV dextrose for hypoglycaemia, (4) identification and treatment of the precipitating cause (gastroenteritis). Once stabilised, taper to oral hydrocortisone at double the usual dose, resume fludrocortisone, and arrange an emergency injection kit and sick-day education. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines - Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis is a life-threatening emergency that occurs when glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid deficiency results in cardiovascular collapse. Common precipitants include intercurrent illness, surgery, and medication non-adherence. IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat is the cornerstone of treatment, providing both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid activity at high doses. Aggressive IV normal saline resuscitation corrects hypovolaemia and hyponatraemia. Prevention through patient education about sick-day rules and provision of emergency injection kits is essential.
key_points: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat provides both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid activity in crisis
Fludrocortisone is not needed acutely as stress-dose hydrocortisone has sufficient mineralocorticoid effect
Aggressive IV normal saline is critical for hypovolaemia correction
All patients with adrenal insufficiency should have an emergency IM hydrocortisone injection kit at home
explanation.pearls: Patients with Addison disease should be educated on sick day rules: double the oral hydrocortisone dose for febrile illness, triple it for severe illness, and administer IM hydrocortisone 100 mg if unable to take oral medications - then present to the emergency department.
